Subject: Orthopedics
QuestionText: A 60-year-old patient presents with limping gait and left hip pain. What is the likely diagnosis?
Explanation:
• The likely diagnosis in this symptomatic patient is avascular necrosis. It is a degenerative condition
 characterized by the death of cellular components of the bone secondary to an interruption of the
 subchondral blood supply. It affects the epiphysis of long bones at weight-bearing joints.
 • The most common sites for AVN are the femoral head, knee, talus, and humeral head. The hip is the
 most common location overall.
 • The given X-ray shows flattening of the superolateral aspect (the weight-bearing portion) of
 the femoral head.
 • However, MRI is the most sensitive modality for diagnosis and staging AVN

Subject: Orthopedics
QuestionText: What is the probable diagnosis for a patient who complains of left hip pain, with a flexed, adducted, and internally rotated affected limb with noticeable shortening?
Explanation:
• The most likely diagnosis based on the given clinical presentation is B. Posterior hip dislocation.
 • In a posterior hip dislocation, the femoral head is displaced posteriorly out of the acetabulum. This can
 occur as a result of high-energy trauma, such as a road traffic accident.
 • The characteristic clinical findings in a posterior hip dislocation include: Position of the affected limb:
 The limb is typically flexed, adducted (moved towards the midline), and medially rotated (inwardly
 rotated). Obvious shortening: The affected limb may appear shorter compared to the contralateral side
 due to the displacement of the femoral head. Restricted range of motion: The patient may experience
 pain and limited range of motion in the affected hip joint.
 • Position of the affected limb: The limb is typically flexed, adducted (moved towards the midline), and
 medially rotated (inwardly rotated).
 • Obvious shortening: The affected limb may appear shorter compared to the contralateral side due to
 the displacement of the femoral head.
• Restricted range of motion: The patient may experience pain and limited range of motion in the
 affected hip joint.
 • To confirm the diagnosis, imaging studies such as X-rays or CT scans of the hip joint can be
 performed. These imaging modalities will demonstrate the posterior displacement of the femoral head
 in relation to the acetabulum.
 • Position of the affected limb: The limb is typically flexed, adducted (moved towards the midline), and
 medially rotated (inwardly rotated).
 • Obvious shortening: The affected limb may appear shorter compared to the contralateral side due to
 the displacement of the femoral head.
 • Restricted range of motion: The patient may experience pain and limited range of motion in the
 affected hip joint.

Subject: Orthopedics
QuestionText: Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis for a 28-year-old man with backache, morning stiffness, and redness of the eyes?
Explanation:
• Ankylosing spondylitis, is the most likely diagnosis based on the given X-ray image of the spine.
 • Ankylosing spondylitis is a chronic inflammatory condition that primarily affects the axial skeleton,
 particularly the sacroiliac joints and the spine.
 • The X-ray findings seen in ankylosing spondylitis include sacroiliitis, which is characterized by bilateral
 involvement of the sacroiliac joints with erosions, sclerosis, and joint space narrowing.
 • Additionally, ankylosing spondylitis is associated with the development of syndesmophytes, which are
 bony bridges that form between adjacent vertebrae. These syndesmophytes can lead to the classic
 "bamboo spine" appearance on X-ray, where the spine appears fused and rigid.
 Ankylosing spondylitis is a chronic inflammatory condition that primarily affects the axial skeleton, parti
 cularly the sacroiliac joints and the spine.
 • It is more common in young males and has a strong association with HLA-B27.
 • Clinical features include inflammatory back pain, morning stiffness, and limited spinal mobility.
 • Extra-articular manifestations can occur, such as uveitis, aortic regurgitation, and involvement of other
 joints, such as the hips and shoulders.
• Radiographic findings typically include bilateral sacroiliitis, characterized by erosions, sclerosis, and
 joint space narrowing in the sacroiliac joints.
 • The development of syndesmophytes, bony bridges between adjacent vertebrae, leads to bamboo
 spine appearance on X-ray.
 • HLA-B27 testing can aid in the diagnosis, but it is not definitive as some patients with ankylosing
 spondylitis are HLA-B27 negative.
 • Treatment involves a combination of nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drugs (NSAIDs), physical therapy,
 exercise, and in severe cases, biological agents such as TNF-alpha inhibitors may be used.
 • Ankylosing spondylitis is a progressive disease with the potential for spinal deformity and functional
 impairment.

QuestionText: Avascular necrosis of the femur head is due to which of the following artery?
Explanation:
Medial Circumflex Femoral Artery (MCFA): The MCFA is a branch of the deep femoral artery and provi
 des the primary blood supply to the femoral head. Disruption or occlusion of this artery can lead to com
 promised blood flow to the femoral head, resulting in avascular necrosis.
 The diagnosis and management of avascular necrosis of the femoral head typically involve a combinati
 on of clinical evaluation, imaging studies (such as X-rays, MRI, or bone scans), and treatment options r
 anging from conservative measures to surgical interventions, depending on the severity and stage of th
 e condition

 s or jumper's knee, where there is pain or inflammation in the patellar tendon

Subject: Orthopedics
QuestionText: What is the term used to describe the fracture shown in the image, which occurred due to a fall from a significant height?

 in fractures to the anterior and posterior arches. Both the halves are separated at the anterior position
 and posterior position. The front portion being more open than the rear end

QuestionText: A 15-year-old boy was brought to the ER following a motor vehicle collision complaining of hip pain. What will be the next step of management?
Explanation:
• The above X-ray shows characteristics of posterior dislocation of the left hip.
 • Deformity observed is flexion, adduction, and internal rotation of the hip.
 • Shows a history of trauma, pain, swelling, and deformity pointing towards a posterior dislocation.
 • Closed reduction is a non-surgical technique to realign the displaced bone to its normal position.
 Anesthesia given and placed supine on the floor

 Axial pull
 Sound heard
 Traction with hip abducted for 3 weeks
 Hip mobilization exercises

QuestionText: The splint depicted below is—
Explanation:

 • It is also termed as Dennis brown splint.

Subject: Orthopedics
QuestionText: What is the most likely diagnosis for a 35-year-old pregnant woman experiencing back pain after lifting a heavy suitcase?
Explanation:
L4-L5 disc prolapse: The symptoms described in the case, including back pain radiating to th
 e posterolateral aspect of the leg, sensory loss over the anterolateral aspect of the leg, dorsum of the f
 oot, and great toe, and diminished medial hamstring reflex, are consistent with a disc prolapse at the L
 4-L5 level. The L4 spinal nerve root supplies the mentioned sensory distribution, and the diminished m
 edial hamstring reflex indicates involvement of the L4 nerve root.
 The given image is also confirms above
 Therefore, based on the clinical presentation and findings on MRI, the most probable diagnosis is L4-L

Subject: Orthopedics
QuestionText: The patient is a 67-year-old with diabetes who came in with knee swelling, limited mobility, and little to no pain. What is the diagnosis?
Explanation:
• Neuropathic arthropathy of the knee (Charcot knee) is a rare pathology defined by progressive
 destruction of bone and soft tissue in a patient with underlying peripheral neuropathy.
 • Historically, Charcot knee was associated with neurosyphilis, but it has been increasingly described
 as a late stage of diabetes.
 • Presentation :Early Stage: Swelling, warmth, and erythema of the affected joint without significant
 pain. Later Stage: Joint deformities, instability, and potential fractures.
 • Later Stage: Joint deformities, instability, and potential fractures.
 • Diagnosis : Clinical examination, imaging studies (X-rays, MRI, CT scans), and blood tests to identify
 the underlying cause, such as diabetes.
 • Stages : stage 0 (Development): Joint swelling without deformity. Stage 1 (Coalescence):
 Fragmentation and destruction of the joint with debris formation. Stage 2 (Reconstruction):
 Reabsorption of debris and attempts at joint reconstruction. Stage 3 (Reconstitution): Remodeling and
 reconstitution of the joint.
 • Stage 1 (Coalescence): Fragmentation and destruction of the joint with debris formation.
 • Stage 2 (Reconstruction): Reabsorption of debris and attempts at joint reconstruction.
 • Stage 3 (Reconstitution): Remodeling and reconstitution of the joint.
 • Treatment: Offloading: Immobilization with casts or specialized footwear to reduce weight-bearing
 stress. Management of Underlying Cause: Control of diabetes or other contributing factors. Orthopedic
 Interventions: Surgery may be needed in severe cases.
 • Later Stage: Joint deformities, instability, and potential fractures.
 • Stage 1 (Coalescence): Fragmentation and destruction of the joint with debris formation.
 • Stage 2 (Reconstruction): Reabsorption of debris and attempts at joint reconstruction.
• Stage 3 (Reconstitution): Remodeling and reconstitution of the joint.

 Example: Dashboard injury.

QuestionText: The brace shown in the given image is
Explanation:
Given image indeed depicts the Milwaukee brace.
 The Milwaukee brace is a full-torso orthopedic brace commonly used in the treatment of spinal disorder
 s, particularly scoliosis. It extends from the pelvis to the base of the skull and includes a
 metal upright along the back and a neck ring for support. The brace is custom-made and designed to a
 pply corrective forces to the spine, aiming to prevent further progression of spinal curvature.

Subject: Orthopedics
QuestionText: A 79-year-old lady had a fall in the bathroom. Which of the following is the treatment for this condition?
Explanation:
• The X-ray shows a fracture of the neck of the femur (hip fracture). In older patients, especially those
 over 65 years, surgical management with hemiarthroplasty (partial hip replacement) is often the
 preferred treatment for displaced femoral neck fractures.
 • Hemiarthroplasty involves replacing the fractured head of the femur with a prosthetic implant while
 leaving the acetabulum intact. This procedure helps to restore hip function, relieve pain, and allow early
 mobilization.

Subject: Orthopedics
QuestionText: A child comes with a limp and limb shortening. What is the diagnosis?
Explanation:
• The X-ray shows a dislocated hip joint with hip dysplasia and apparent limb shortening.
 • Developmental Dysplasia of the Hip (DDH), also known as congenital hip dysplasia or hip dislocation,
 refers to abnormal development of the hip joint, leading to instability, subluxation, or dislocation of the
 femoral head from the acetabulum. It commonly presents with a limp and limb length discrepancy

Subject: Orthopedics
QuestionText: A 40-year-old man is suffering from difficulty bending forward with stiffness of the spine and reduced chest movements. What is the likely diagnosis?
Explanation:
• Ankylosing spondylitis is a chronic inflammatory condition that primarily affects the spine. It is
 characterized by inflammation of the spinal joints, leading to stiffness, reduced mobility, and pain. The
 symptoms typically worsen over time and may result in a forward-bending posture.
 • The CT image provided, showing characteristic findings of ankylosing spondylitis, supports the
 diagnosis. In ankylosing spondylitis, CT scans often reveal syndesmophytes, which are bony
 outgrowths that bridge adjacent vertebrae and result in the formation of a "bamboo spine" appearance

Subject: Orthopedics
QuestionText: The brace shown in the following image is called a __
Explanation:
• These are various types of Thoraco-lumbosacral orthoses (TLSO).
 • The indications include scoliosis, Scheuerman disease, and vertebral fractures.
 • The braces have standardized and characteristic designs which makes it easy to identify them.
 •  The image in the question shows a Taylor brace. It has Axillary straps to help control the upper
 thoracic region. Due to its long posterior bands, it limits the extension and flexion of the spine.

Subject: Orthopedics
QuestionText: A 25-year-old male presented with pain and swelling of his right knee. What is the diagnosis?
Explanation:
The answer can be deduced by carefully observing the various structures in the x-ray: patella, distal fe
 mur, and proximal tibia.
 The radiograph shows the lateral view of the knee joint with a displaced transverse fracture of the patel
 la. The distal part of the femur and the proximal part of the tibia are normal.
 • Patellar fractures can result from direct or indirect forces
 • Direct injury can cause a comminuted fracture or stellate fracture. Sudden quadriceps contraction can
 lead to a two-part fracture.
 • Clinical features: Pain, swelling, and tenderness over the knee. Crepitus in comminuted fractures, the
 gap between fracture fragments in displaced fractures Inability to lift the leg with full knee extension
 (extensor lag) due to disruption of the extensor apparatus
 • Bruises over the front of the knee and swollen knee due to haemarthrosis
 Diagnosis can be confirmed with an x-ray.
 Treatment: Tension band wiring is the treatment of choice. Occasionally, partial or total patellectomy m
 ay be required for comminuted fractures

Subject: Orthopedics
QuestionText: Which of the following is the most probable diagnosis for a 9-year-old boy who has pain and limping for the past 6 months?
Explanation:
Perthes disea • Both SCFE and Perthe’s disease present with pain and limping. DDH and Congenital coxa vara
 present with complaints of a painless limp.
 • The diagnosis can be narrowed down to Perthes disease based on the age of the patient (5-10 years
 age group), complaint of pain and limping, with abduction (especially in flexion), and internal rotation is
 limited. Additionally, the x-ray shows a deformed femoral head. The acetabulum is normal (ruling out
 DDH).
 • Slipped capital femoral epiphysis (SCFE) can be ruled out because the patient has normal weight and
 height SCFE is commonly seen in the age group of 10-15 years and is associated with endocrine
 disorders. The child is usually obese.
 • Children with DDH usually have an abnormal gait as soon as they start walking. But the gait is
 painless. Similarly, congenital coxa vara also presents as a painless limpase presents with hip pain, limping, and restricted motion in children, often with good overall health.

Explanation: Extensor hallucis longus: The marked tendon corresponds to this muscle, which extends the great toe and assists in dorsiflexion of the ankle. It originates from the fibula and inserts onto the distal phalanx of the great toe, playing a significant role in foot movements.

Subject: Orthopedics
QuestionText: Please identify the labeled tarsal bone depicted in the image provided.
Explanation: Navicular: This is one of the seven tarsal bones in the foot, located medially in the midfoot, positioned between the talus and the three cuneiform bones, playing a key role in foot structure.

Subject: Orthopedics
QuestionText: The image displayed represents a femur bone. What muscles are connected to point "A"?
Explanation: Iliopsoas: The lesser trochanter of the femur serves as the insertion point for the iliopsoas muscle, which is composed of the iliacus and psoas major. This muscle is the primary flexor of the hip joint and is essential for movements involving hip flexion and external rotation.

Subject: Medmaven
QuestionText: A baby presented with abdominal pain. On examination, a mass is palpated in the left lumbar region. A barium enema is done, and the image is given below. What is the likely diagnosis?
Explanation: Intussusception is a medical condition that affects newborns and young children, causing stomach pain and other symptoms. It occurs when a section of the intestine folds in on itself, resulting in obstruction and blockage. Diagnosis is typically made through imaging, such as a barium enema. Treatment may involve an enema or surgery, depending on the severity of the case. (Reference: Nelson Textbook of Pediatrics)

Subject: Medmaven
QuestionText: What is the diagnosis for a neonate who was brought in with a complaint of frothiness from the mouth and respiratory distress, as shown in the provided X-ray image?
Explanation: In esophageal atresia (EA), the upper esophagus does not connect (atresia) to the lower esophagus and stomach. Saliva, liquids fed to the infant, or digestive fluids may enter the windpipe through the tracheoesophageal fistula, leading to coughing, respiratory distress, and a bluish appearance of the skin or lips (cyanosis). (Reference: Nelson Textbook of Pediatrics)

Subject: Medmaven
QuestionText: What is the likely diagnosis for a case of an 11-year-old boy with intentional tremors, poor scholastic performance, hepatomegaly, and an eye finding shown in the image, when his sister also presents similar complaints?
Explanation: In the image provided, the eye finding associated with Wilson's disease is Kayser-Fleischer rings. These are golden-brown or greenish-brown rings that appear in the cornea due to the deposition of copper. Kayser-Fleischer rings are best visualized using a slit-lamp examination and are highly suggestive of Wilson's disease. (Reference: Harrison's Principles of Internal Medicine)

Subject: Orthopedics
QuestionText: What is the probable diagnosis for a child brought to the orthopedics OPD with a lower limb deformity and hyperpigmented skin lesions, based on the presented X-ray of her thigh?
Explanation: Fibrous dysplasia is characterized by a ground-glass appearance on X-rays due to abnormal fibrous tissue replacing normal bone. It can present with lower limb deformities and skin lesions, making it the most probable diagnosis in this case. Non-ossifying fibroma appears as a well-defined radiolucent lesion; Paget's disease involves older individuals with bone pain and enlargement; Osteogenesis imperfecta involves fragile bones but not hyperpigmented lesions.

Subject: Orthopedics
QuestionText: Which test confirms the diagnosis of Gout?
Explanation: Synovial fluid analysis is the gold standard for diagnosing gout. It confirms the presence of monosodium urate crystals in the joint fluid, leading to inflammation. While serum uric acid levels are often elevated in gout, they are not definitive for diagnosis as they can be normal in some cases. Urine uric acid levels and X-ray changes are also not specific for gout diagnosis.

Subject: Orthopedics
QuestionText: In a 13-week pregnancy, an intrauterine scan revealed a fetus displaying fractures in multiple long bones. What is typically linked to this discovery?
Explanation: Osteogenesis imperfecta is characterized by brittle bones leading to multiple fractures, even in utero. It is a genetic disorder affecting collagen production. Achondroplasia affects bone growth but typically does not cause fractures; cretinism involves developmental issues from hypothyroidism; Marfan syndrome affects connective tissue and does not present with fetal fractures.

Subject: Orthopedics
QuestionText: What is the likely diagnosis for a 55-year-old woman who presents with lower back pain? Please refer to the provided radiographic image of her lumbosacral spine.
Explanation: Osteoporosis is characterized by loss of bone density and structural deterioration, commonly causing vertebral compression fractures. The described radiographic findings are consistent with osteoporosis. Ankylosing spondylitis leads to spinal stiffness and fusion; osteomalacia involves impaired mineralization; renal osteodystrophy relates to chronic kidney disease and does not specifically fit the findings.

Subject: Orthopedics
QuestionText: What treatment would you provide to a 60-year-old female patient who had a Colles' fracture 8 months ago, has a T score of -2.5, attained menopause at 52 years, and is currently not experiencing any symptoms, when she visits the orthopedics outpatient department?
Explanation: Alendronate, a bisphosphonate, is the first-line treatment for osteoporosis indicated by a T-score of -2.5. It inhibits bone resorption and reduces fracture risk. Hormone replacement therapy is not the first choice due to side effects; calcium and vitamin D are not sufficient alone for osteoporosis treatment; a repeat DEXA scan is unnecessary given the clear diagnosis.

Subject: Orthopedics
QuestionText: What would be your diagnosis for a 60-year-old female patient who complains of low back pain and shows the following findings on a lumbar CT scan?
Explanation: The findings of biconcave vertebrae suggest osteoporosis, commonly presenting in older women with low back pain. Spondylolisthesis shows vertebral displacement; discitis involves inflammation of discs; Pott's spine indicates spinal tuberculosis, which has different imaging characteristics.

Subject: Orthopedics
QuestionText: A 4-year-old child was brought to the OPD with a painless deformity. A radiograph of the child's legs is shown below. The most common cause of this deformity is____.
Explanation: Rickets, caused by vitamin D deficiency, leads to bowing of the legs (genu varum) in children, often painless. Skeletal dysplasia involves various bone growth disorders; scurvy is related to vitamin C deficiency; Blount disease involves tibial deformities but is less common than rickets.

Subject: Monitoring In Anesthesia
QuestionText: What is the most effective way to verify the placement of the endotracheal tube in a 30-year-old male patient who has been intubated for surgery by a final-year resident?
Explanation: End-tidal CO2 concentration is the best method to confirm endotracheal tube placement, as it directly measures CO2 in exhaled breath, indicating proper placement in the trachea. X-ray and auscultation can be misleading, and chest rise alone is not definitive.

Subject: Monitoring In Anesthesia
QuestionText: What could be the probable reason for the observed result in the capnograph of a patient who is intubated and undergoing controlled ventilation?
Explanation: Spontaneous respiratory effort can disrupt the CO2 waveform during controlled ventilation, leading to a curare notch. Esophageal intubation would show little to no CO2, and bronchospasm would alter the waveform but not create a curare notch. Inspiration with cardiac oscillations would cause minor fluctuations, not a distinct notch.

Subject: Monitoring In Anesthesia
QuestionText: What is the most frequently employed method for monitoring the level of anesthesia?
Explanation: Bispectral index (BIS) is the most commonly used method for monitoring anesthesia depth, providing a numerical value based on EEG activity. Other methods, while useful, are not as widely employed in clinical practice.

Subject: Monitoring In Anesthesia
QuestionText: What is the parameter being assessed by the device depicted in the provided image?
Explanation: The device is a pulse oximeter, which measures oxygen saturation in the blood, indicating the percentage of hemoglobin saturated with oxygen. It does not directly measure oxygen content, partial pressure, or inspired oxygen levels.

Subject: Monitoring In Anesthesia
QuestionText: Cardiac output can be determined using the following except?
Explanation: Capnography is not a method for determining cardiac output; it measures CO2 levels in exhaled air. Thermal dilution, esophageal doppler, and echocardiography are valid techniques for measuring cardiac output.

Subject: Monitoring In Anesthesia
QuestionText: Which of the following methods cannot be used to determine cardiac output?
Explanation: capnography does not measure cardiac output, while the other methods are valid techniques for determining it.

QuestionText: What is the diagnosis of a 40-year-old patient who presents with a significant history of mood disorder, as depicted in the provided image?
Explanation:
The finding of the quick cycling problem depends on the accompanying standards:
 • The patient has had at least four episodes of craziness, hypomania, or sorrow in a year's time
 span.
 • A substance or another ailment does not bring about episodes of craziness, hypomania, or
 sadness.
 • The episodes of madness, hypomania, or discouragement cause huge impedance in friendships,
 work-related, or other significant areas of life.
 The patient in the picture meets these measures. She has had at least four episodes of lunacy, hy
 pomania, or wretchedness over a year. A substance or another ailment does not bring about these
 episodes. Moreover, these episodes have caused critical disability in her social, work-related, and 
other significant areas of life.
 A rapid cycling disorder's symptoms can be extremely disruptive to a person's life. They can make i
 t hard to keep relationships, work, and take care of yourself. The symptoms can be managed, and l
 ife quality can be improved with the help of effective treatments.

Subject: Pediatrics
QuestionText: What condition is the banana sign observed in?
Explanation: Correct Option A: Spina bifida The "banana sign" is typically seen in the imaging evaluation of fetuses with spina bifida. Let's explore each option in more detail: 1045 → Spina Bifida: Spina bifida is a congenital defect where the neural tube, which develops into the spinal cord and spine, does not close properly during fetal development. The banana sign refers to a curved appearance of the cerebellum, resembling a banana, which can be visualized on ultrasound or magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) of the fetal brain. This finding is highly suggestive of spina bifida. Incorrect Options Option B: NCC (Neural Crest Cell) Defects: Neural crest cells play a crucial role in the development of various structures in the body, including the nervous system, face, and heart. While neural crest cell de fects can manifest in different ways, the specific term "banana sign" is not associated with NCC defects . Option C: Omphalocele: Omphalocele is a congenital abdominal wall defect where some of the abdomi nal organs protrude through an opening in the umbilical area and are covered by a sac. The banana sign is not typically observed in cases of omphalocele. Option D: Anencephaly: Anencephaly is a severe congenital malformation where a major portion of the brain, skull, and scalp do not develop properly. The banana sign is not specific to anencephaly and is not typically associated with this condition. In summary, the banana sign is most commonly seen in cases of spina bifida, where the cerebellum ex hibits a curved appearance resembling a banana.

Subject: Pediatrics
QuestionText: A pediatric patient arrives at the hospital complaining of several abnormal fractures, and the X-ray shows the image shown below. The blood tests indicate low levels of phosphorus and calcium, normal levels of Vitamin D, and increased levels of PTH. What is the most likely diagnosis for this patient?
Explanation: Correct option: A → The description of multiple pathological fractures, low phosphorus and calcium levels, normal Vitamin D levels, and elevated PTH along with the X-ray findings is consistent with osteopetrosis. Osteopetrosis is a genetic disorder characterized by defective osteoclast function, leading to increased bone density and susceptibility to fractures. Incorrect options: Option B: Osteomalacia: Osteomalacia is characterized by softening of the bones due to vitamin D deficiency or impaired metabolism. However, in this case, the Vitamin D levels are normal. Option C: Osteosclerosis: Osteosclerosis refers to abnormal hardening or increased density of the bon es, but it does not typically present with multiple pathological fractures. Option D: Histiocytosis X: Histiocytosis X (also known as Langerhans cell histiocytosis) is a group of di sorders characterized by abnormal proliferation of certain immune cells called Langerhans cells. It doe s not typically present with the findings described in the question.

Subject: Pediatrics
QuestionText: The x-ray image of a child with muscle weakness and failure to thrive is attached below. What is the likely diagnosis?
Explanation: Correct Option B: Rickets 1048 → Rickets is a condition that primarily affects growing children and is caused by a deficiency in vitamin D, calcium, or phosphate. → Rickets primarily affect the growing ends of the long bones, including those in the hand. Radiographically, widened or irregular growth plates in the phalanges (finger bones) and metacarpals (bones of the palm) can be seen. → The metaphyses near the growth plates may show a cupped or flared appearance. → This means that the ends of the bones appear wider and more irregular than the shaft of the bone.

Subject: Microbiology
QuestionText: What is the probable diagnosis for a farmer who has a swollen foot and multiple sinuses, as depicted in the image provided?
Explanation:
• The swollen foot and multiple discharging sinuses are highly suggestive of Mycetoma.
 • It is also known as the Madura's foot.
 • Mycetoma is a chronic granulomatous infection of the subcutaneous tissues and bones that can
 cause a swollen foot and multiple sinuses. 
• It is usually caused by bacteria such as Actinomycetes or fungi such as Madurella, which are found in
 the soil..

Subject: Microbiology
QuestionText: What is the most probable diagnosis for a farmer who has a wart-like skin growth on his foot, as shown in the provided histopathological image?
Explanation:
• The histopathological image shows a characteristic "copper penny" appearance of sclerotic bodies,
 which is consistent with the diagnosis of chromoblastomycosis. 
• Chromoblastomycosis is a chronic fungal infection caused by dematiaceous (pigmented) fungi,
 commonly affecting the skin of the feet and legs of farmers and agricultural workers. 
• The infection typically presents as a warty or verrucous lesion that gradually enlarges and becomes
 more nodular.

Subject: Microbiology
QuestionText: A cattle farmer presented with a lesion on the arm as shown below. Gram staining showed gram-positive rods as shown below. Identify the organism.
Explanation:
• The given clinical picture is of Bacillus anthracis
 • A gram-positive rod with square ends and a central spore “Bamboo-stick appearance” along with the
 presentation of blackish eschar lesions in farmers suggests the organism belongs to the genus
 Bacillus. It is a classical presentation of Bacillus anthracis.
 • Painless papule surrounded by vesicles further ulcer with black eschar given in image (painless,
 necrotic) which uncommonly progresses to bacteremia and death.
 Painless papule surrounded by vesicles further ulcer with black eschar given in image (painless, necrot
 ic) which uncommonly progresses to bacteremia and death

QuestionText: Identify the pathognomonic structure in the slide given below.
Explanation:

 thick-walled, single, or multicellular clusters called sclerotic or muriform bodies. There is remarkable

Subject: Microbiology
QuestionText: The cell wall depicted below is most likely to belong to which of the following organisms?
Explanation:
• Aspergillus fumigatus is a fungus which differ from bacteria by having rigid cell walls containing chitin,
