Subject: Microbiology
QuestionText: What is the probable diagnosis for a 35-year-old patient who is HIV-positive and exhibits mucosal lesions in the mouth, as depicted in the provided image, with the presence of bugging yeasts and pseudohyphae observed under microscopy?
Explanation:
• The likely diagnosis for a 35-year-old HIV-positive patient with mucosal lesions in the mouth and the
 presence of budding yeasts and pseudohyphae on microscopy is oral candidiasis.
 • Oral candidiasis, also known as oral thrush, is a fungal infection caused by the overgrowth of Candida
 species, particularly Candida albicans, in the oral cavity.
 • It is common in individuals with compromised immune systems, such as those with HIV/AIDS.
 • Microscopic examination of a sample from the mucosal lesions would reveal the presence of budding
 yeasts (small circular structures) and pseudohyphae (elongated structures with constrictions or
 constrictions between the budding cells). This characteristic appearance confirms the diagnosis of oral
candidiasis.

Subject: Microbiology
QuestionText: What is the probable microorganism causing the pruritic hypopigmented macules and confluent patches over the chest in a 24-year-old female? The skin scraping and KOH preparation exhibit numerous yeast-like cells and short branch filaments, resembling the appearance of 'Spaghetti and meatballs' as depicted in the image provided.
Explanation:
• Yeasts of the genus Malassezia, formerly known as Pityrosporum, are lipophilic yeasts, which are a
 part of the normal skin flora (microbiome).
 • The species distribution on the skin and the pathogenetic potential of the yeast vary between different
 Malassezia-related diseases, such as head and neck dermatitis, seborrheic dermatitis, pityriasis
 versicolor, and Malassezia folliculitis.
 • Different sampling methods used for the diagnosis of Malassezia include tape stripping, skin scraping,
 swabs, and contact plates.
 • Malassezia is recognized by the detection of characteristic unipolar budding yeasts and accompanied
 by short hyphae, thus the so-called spaghetti and meatballs appearance

Subject: Microbiology
QuestionText: What is the probable causative agent based on the patient's symptoms of fever, difficulty in breathing, rapid heartbeat, and palpitations, along with the detection of vegetation in the septal leaflets through echocardiography and the findings observed in the tissue sections?
Explanation:
• The clinical presentation of fever, dyspnea, palpitations, and tachycardia, along with
 echocardiographic findings of vegetation in the septal leaflets, suggests infective endocarditis.
 • Fungal endocarditis is a rare but serious complication of infective endocarditis, and Aspergillus
 species are one of the fungal pathogens associated with this condition.
 • The histopathological findings show uniform, branching septate hyphae of Aspergillus in tissue
 stained with Gomori methenamine silver, consistent with Aspergillus infection.
 • The hyphae of Aspergillus species are hyaline, septate, acute angled, and uniform in width and
 branch dichotomously.
OptionA: A. Pigmented septate hyphae at 90 degrees, Mucormycosis
OptionB: B. Right angle branching septate hyphae, Fusarium
OptionC: C. Acute angle branching hyphae, Rhizopus
OptionD: D. Acute angle branching septate hyphae, Aspergillus

Subject: Microbiology
QuestionText: Please determine the dermatophyte by examining the macroconidia displayed in the accompanying image.
Explanation:
These infections are known as dermatophytosis or ringworm. The options are:
 Option A: Trichophyton: Trichophyton is a genus of fungi that includes several species capable of causi
 ng infections in humans and animals. On a smear, Trichophyton species typically appear as septate hy
 phae (long filamentous structures with cross-walls) and may also show conidia (asexual spores) or arth
 roconidia (fragmented hyphae). The shape and arrangement of the hyphae can vary depending on the 
species.
 Option B: Microsporum: Microsporum is another genus of dermatophyte fungi that can cause ringworm
 infections. On a smear, Microsporum species often show both macroconidia (large, multicellular spore
 s) and microconidia (small, single-celled spores). The macroconidia are typically thick-walled and spind
 le-shaped, while the microconidia are more rounded or oval.
 Option C: Epidermophyton: Epidermophyton is a
 genus of dermatophyte fungi that primarily infects the skin, hair, and nails. On a smear, Epidermophyto
 n species usually appear as septate hyphae, similar to Trichophyton species. However, they may not p
 roduce conidia or arthroconidia, making their identification based on smear morphology alone more ch
 allenging

Subject: Microbiology
QuestionText: Which of the following tests is used to identify ________?
Explanation:
• Positive identification of the test shown in the image, which is the germ tube test, is A. Candida
 albicans.
 • The germ tube test is a laboratory diagnostic test used to identify Candida species, specifically
 Candida albicans. This test is based on the ability of Candida albicans to produce germ tubes, which
 are elongated outgrowths from yeast cells.
 • When Candida albicans is grown in specific growth media under appropriate conditions, it exhibits
 germ tube formation. The presence of germ tubes is considered a characteristic feature of Candida
 albicans, and it helps distinguish it from other Candida species.

Subject: Microbiology
QuestionText: A skin biopsy sample was taken and sent from histopathological examination. The characteristic microscopic body as shown in the image was seen. Which of the following could be the most likely diagnosis?
Explanation:
Chromoblastomycosis: Chromoblastomycosis is a chronic fungal infection caused by various
 dematiaceous fungi. The characteristic microscopic bodies seen in the image, known as Medlar bodie
 s or copper pennies, are typical of chromoblastomycosis. Therefore, chromoblastomycosis is the most l
 ikely diagnosis based on the given information.

Subject: National Health Program
QuestionText: A baby presents for a routine examination in the outpatient department. A growth chart is utilized to record the baby's weight, which falls within the 85th - 95th percentile range. What is the present condition of the child?
Explanation:
• Growth charts are used to monitor a child's growth and development over time. These charts provide
 a range of values called percentiles, which indicate how a child's measurements compare to those of
 other children of the same age and sex. The 85th-95th percentile range is considered overweight,
 indicating that the child is heavier than 85-95% of children of the same age and sex.
 • However, it is important to note that growth charts alone cannot determine if a child is obese or at risk
 for obesity. Body mass index (BMI) is a better indicator of body fatness and can be calculated using the
 child's weight and height. If the child's BMI falls within the 85th-95th percentile range, they are
 considered overweight, while a BMI above the 95th percentile is classified as obesity.
OptionD: Interpretation cannot be made without BMI

Subject: National Health Program
QuestionText: Which disease is targeted under the National Vector Borne Disease Control Program, with the insect depicted below acting as the vector? (or) The insect shown below is a vector for a disease that is targeted under the National vector borne disease control programme. Identify the disease.
Explanation:
• The insect shown in the figure above is culex mosquito which is the vector for Japanese encephalitis.
 Japanese encephalitis is a viral disease that affects the brain and is transmitted by infected
 mosquitoes, primarily Culex species. It is targeted under the National vector-borne disease control
 programs in regions where the disease is endemic.

Subject: National Health Program
QuestionText: The World Health Organization (WHO) has defined blindness as a condition where the level of visual acuity is below ____.
Explanation:
• WHO (World Health Organization) defines blindness as visual acuity less than 3/60.
 • Visual acuity is a measure of the clarity and sharpness of vision. It is typically expressed as a fraction,
 with the numerator representing the distance at which the person is standing from the visual chart and
 the denominator representing the distance at which a person with normal vision can see the same
 chart.
 • In the given options, the visual acuity less than 3/60 is the correct threshold for defining blindness
 according to WHO. This means that a person with visual acuity less than 3/60 is considered to be blind.
 It indicates severe impairment of vision, where the person can only see at a distance of 3 meters or less
 what a person with normal vision can see clearly at 60 meters.
 • Therefore, the correct answer is: 3/60

Subject: National Health Program
QuestionText: As a medical officer assigned to rural service, your role includes overseeing the implementation of preventive measures against malaria in your region. Based on the program's logo displayed below, you should provide guidance on all of the following aspects, except for:
Explanation:
Indoor residual sprays are not suitable for vector control in rural areas: This statement is inco
 rrect. Indoor residual spraying (IRS) is considered an effective vector control measure, including in rura
 l areas. IRS involves spraying insecticides on the interior walls of houses to kill mosquitoes and reduce
 their population. It is an important component of integrated malaria control programs.
OptionA: Two rounds of spraying with DDT should be done to provide protection during the entire transmission season.
OptionB: Long lasting insecticide nest (LLIN) should be provided in areas with annual parasite index ≥5.
OptionC: Indoor residual sprays are not suitable for vector control in rural areas.
OptionD: In case of DDT resistance, 3 rounds of spraying with Malathion should be done to provide protection during the entire transmission season.

Subject: National Health Program
QuestionText: Identify the image:
Explanation:
• The given image is Lepromatous Leprosy. It is one of the types of Leprosy and is characterized by
 widespread, poorly defined, shiny-surfaced, hypopigmented, and erythematous macules with normal
 sensation, Typical leonine features include a thickened forehead, lost brow hairs, and eyelashes
 (madarosis), a distorted nose, and thickened earlobes.

Subject: National Health Program
QuestionText: Which of the following diseases are included in the scheme, which has the logo as given below?
Explanation:
• The logo given in the image is of the National Programme for Prevention and Control of Cancer,
 Diabetes, Cardiovascular Diseases and Stroke (NPCDCS). Under this programme, the included
 diseases are CVD, Cancer, Diabetes, and Stroke.

Subject: National Health Program
QuestionText: Please recognize the logo displayed in the provided image.
Explanation:
• The given logo is the logo of Suraksha Clinic which is under the National AIDS Control Programme.
 Suraksha Clinic (previously Designated STI/RTI Clinics) is an STI/RTI clinic chain. The National AIDS
 Control Organisation founded these clinics to provide improved healthcare and to combat HIV/AIDS in
 India.

Subject: Pediatrics
QuestionText: What is the conversion product of bilirubin during phototherapy?
Explanation: During phototherapy, bilirubin is converted into lumirubin, a water-soluble form of bilirubin that can be easily excreted through urine.

Subject: Pediatrics
QuestionText: The procedure displayed below is?
Explanation: The image depicts a nasogastric tube insertion, which is used for feeding and gastric decompression in infants who cannot feed orally.

Subject: Neoplasia
QuestionText: A male patient with hemoptysis. On examination, a hilar mass was present. The histopathological image is shown below. Immunohistochemistry revealed that the cells were positive for p40. What is the diagnosis?
Explanation:
• The patient's presentation with hemoptysis and a hilar mass showing keratin pearls on histopathology
 and cells showing p40 positivity on immunohistochemistry is suggestive of squamous cell carcinoma of
 lung

Subject: Neoplasia
QuestionText: A 7-year-old child presents with fever, erythematous rash, and bone pain. An X-ray of the skull shows multiple lytic lesions. A skin biopsy shows prominent nuclear grooves with eosinophils, as shown in the image below. Immunohistochemistry for S100 and CD1a is also shown in the images below. What would be the diagnosis?
Explanation:
• The clinical presentation of fever, erythematous rash, and bone pain in a 7-year-old child with multiple
 lytic lesions on X-ray and skin biopsy showing prominent nuclear grooves and eosinophils and
 immunohistochemistry for S100 and CD1a suggests Langerhans cell histiocytosis.
 • Langerhans cell histiocytosis is a rare disorder characterized by the accumulation and proliferation of
 Langerhans cells, a type of dendritic cell.
 • The disease can affect any age group but is most commonly seen in children between the ages of 1
 and 15 years.
 • The most common sites of involvement are bone, skin, lymph nodes, and lungs.

Subject: Neoplasia
QuestionText: Which of the following is associated with the cell given below?
Explanation:
• The image shows a Reed-Sternberg cell.
 • They have distinct morphological features, including a bilobed or multinucleated appearance,
 prominent nucleoli, and abundant cytoplasm.
 • Hodgkin lymphoma is a type of lymphoma characterized by the presence of Reed-Sternberg cells
 within the lymph nodes.
• It commonly affects young adults and presents with painless lymphadenopathy, often in the cervical
 region.

Subject: Neoplasia
QuestionText: A 3-year-old boy was brought by his parents with concerns about being increasingly clumsy for the past 6 months. On examination, there is leukocoria with an absence of the red reflex in the left eye. As further workup reveals a tumor, the eye is enucleated, and the microscopic appearance is as shown in the image. Molecular analysis of the tumor indicates a loss of cell cycle control in the tumor cells. What is the most likely diagnosis?
Explanation:
• Retinoblastoma is the most common ocular neoplasm in children.
 • The usual age for clinical presentation in non-familial cases is 1 year, and the usual clinical
 appearance is leukocoria.
 • Histopathologically, retinoblastoma is composed of small blue cells intermingled with anastomosing
 pools of pink necrosis.
 • More differentiated retinoblastomas often exhibit Flexner-Wintersteiner rosettes.

Subject: Nephrology
QuestionText: A 10-year-old presents with edema and anasarca. A diagnosis of minimal change disease is made. Which of the following are true about this condition?
Explanation: Minimal change disease presents with podocyte effacement and responds well to steroids. Reference: Harrison’s Principles of Internal Medicine.
OptionA: Light microscopy shows effacement of podocytes

Subject: Nephrology
QuestionText:
What is the diagnosis of a 5-year-old child who initially had edema of the face that later developed into generalized edema? The urine analysis revealed significant proteinuria, while light microscopy
results were normal. However, electron microscopy indicated effacement of podocyte foot processes.
Explanation: Minimal-change disease is the most common cause in children and is confirmed via electron microscopy. Reference: American Journal of Kidney Diseases.

Subject: Nephrology
QuestionText:
What investigation can be employed to determine the underlying diagnosis in a 3-year-old boy with recurrent UTIs The mother reports that he urinates
frequently but has a weak urinary stream and dribbling of urine. Upon examination, a palpable bladderis detected.
Explanation: Micturating cystourethrography helps diagnose urinary obstruction or reflux. Reference: Journal of Pediatric Nephrology.

Subject: Nephrology
QuestionText: What is the probable diagnosis for a male patient taking diuretics who presents with weakness and an ECG revealing flattened T waves and prominent U waves?
Explanation: The most likely diagnosis is hypokalemia. Diuretics can cause potassium loss leading to hypokalemia, which is characterized by ECG changes like flat T waves and prominent U waves. Symptoms can include muscle weakness, fatigue, muscle spasm, and palpitations.

Subject: Nephrology
QuestionText: What is the likely diagnosis for a patient who complains of pain in the flank region accompanied by hematuria, and has multiple calcifications (stones) observed in both kidneys on X-ray examination?
Explanation: Flank pain, hematuria, and multiple calcifications on X-ray suggest renal calculi (kidney stones). Further imaging may confirm the diagnosis.

Subject: Nephrology
QuestionText: What acid-base imbalance is most likely to be observed in a middle-aged man with chronic renal failure on dialysis who experiences sudden collapse in the emergency department and exhibits labored breathing, as evidenced by tall, tented T waves on the ECG?
Explanation: The pH is low at 7.14 (acidemia), with low pCO2 suggesting respiratory alkalosis and a very low HCO3- indicating severe metabolic acidosis. Tall T waves on ECG suggest hyperkalemia, often associated with metabolic acidosis.

Subject: Nephrology
QuestionText: Please identify the urinary crystal displayed in the image provided.
Explanation: Calcium oxalate stones form due to high levels of calcium and oxalate in urine, often linked to dietary intake. Symptoms include severe pain and difficulty urinating. Other types like calcium phosphate, uric acid, and cysteine stones have different causes and characteristics.

Subject: Nephrology
QuestionText: A 30-year-old woman presents to the OPD with palpitation and weight loss. On examination of her legs the following lesion was found which on applying pressure, the indentation does not persist on release. ECG shows sinus rhythm. What is the probable cause of the edema?
Explanation: Nephrotic syndrome leads to increased glomerular permeability, resulting in protein loss and edema. The clinical presentation of palpitations and weight loss suggests fluid retention due to hypoalbuminemia. Non-pitting edema is consistent with nephrotic syndrome. Other options do not align with the described symptoms.

Subject: Nephrology
QuestionText: Which of the following symptoms will be observed in an 8-year-old girl who suddenly develops fever, reduced urine output, nausea, dark urine, and swollen eyes? The parents also mention that she had similar facial lesions as shown in the image below, but they are currently improving.
Explanation: The clinical presentation suggests poststreptococcal glomerulonephritis (PSGN), with RBCs in urine (hematuria) due to immune complex deposition. PSGN typically presents with hematuria and mild proteinuria. Other symptoms such as liver enzyme derangement are not associated with PSGN.

Subject: Nephrology
QuestionText: What is the probable diagnosis for a patient who has symptoms of frequent urination, nocturia, and enuresis, along with a 24-hour urine volume of 7 liters, a urine osmolarity of 260 mOsm/L, an ADH assay result of 0.8 pg/ml, and a brain MRI showing no bright spot on T1 weighted images?
Explanation: Hemoglobin, found in red blood cells, binds to oxygen in the lungs and carries it to tissues throughout the body, playing a crucial role in oxygen transport.

Subject: Nephrology
QuestionText: A 26-year-old female named Rinku Devi, who works as a labourer, arrived with complaints of abdominal pain, nausea, and restlessness for the past 4 hours. She also mentioned having experienced two episodes of blood in her urine. An ultrasound scan revealed a 3x3 cm stone in her right renal pelvis, with no signs of hydronephrosis. What would be the most suitable procedure for treating this patient?
Explanation: The hypothalamus plays a critical role in regulating hunger and satiety, influencing appetite through hormonal and neural signals.

Subject: Physiology
QuestionText: Which statement accurately identifies the nerve action potential curve provided?
Explanation: The given graph represents an action potential. Point B to D represents the refractory period, which includes both the absolute and relative refractory periods, during which a second action potential is either impossible or more difficult to trigger.
OptionA: The threshold point is at A
OptionB: At point E, the nerve is more excitable
OptionC: Point C to D is due to the opening of Na+ and closure of K+ channels
OptionD: Point B to D is a refractory period

Subject: Physiology
QuestionText: The image shows changes in membrane potential during an action potential. The region marked between 4 to 5 is due to ______________.
Explanation: The region marked between 4 and 5 is due to the efflux of K+. This sharp rise of membrane potential is known as an upstroke, and the portion above levels 3 & 5 is called overshoot.

Subject: Physiology
QuestionText: A and B in the graph below represent which of the following ions, in terms of permeability for action potential generation?
Explanation: During an action potential, Na+ influx causes depolarization, followed by K+ efflux leading to repolarization. Thus, A is Na+ and B is K+.
OptionA: A is Na+, B is K+
OptionB: A is Na+, B is Ca2+
OptionC: A is K+, B is Ca2+
OptionD: A is Ca2+, B is K+

Subject: Physiology
QuestionText: The given image displays a basic muscle twitch and its corresponding time trace, which was recorded in a frog exhibiting the lowest frequency required for tetanization. (The time trace was obtained using a tuning fork with a frequency of 100 Hz.)
Explanation: The image shows a simple muscle twitch along with the time trace recorded in a frog with the minimum tetanizing frequency. The time tracer was made with a 100 Hz tuning fork, which means it vibrates 100 times per second. The tuning fork creates a regular oscillating signal that is used to measure the frequency of the muscle twitch. By observing the time trace, we can count the number of muscle twitches that occur within a specific time interval. In this case, we can see that there are 5 muscle twitches recorded in the time trace, and the time trace is based on the 100 Hz tuning fork. To determine the frequency of the muscle twitches, we need to divide the number of twitches by the time interval. Since the tuning fork vibrates 100 times per second (100 Hz), and there are 5 twitches recorded, we can calculate the frequency as: Frequency = Number of twitches / Time interval = 5 / 100 = 0.05 Hz. Therefore, the correct option is "20 per second," indicating that the muscle twitches occur at a frequency of 20 per second.

Subject: Physiology
QuestionText: Identify the ions A and B based on the conductance graph of a neuron shown below:
Explanation: Sodium and Potassium are two essential ions involved in the generation and conduction of action potentials in neurons. Sodium plays a crucial role in depolarization, while Potassium contributes to repolarization and restoring the resting membrane potential. The conductance graph shows a rapid increase in conductance for ion A, followed by a gradual increase and stable conductance for ion B. This pattern matches the behavior of sodium (Na) and potassium (K) ions during an action potential.

Subject: Neurology
QuestionText: In the optic pathway, what are the initial neurons?
Explanation: Rods and cones are the photoreceptors that initiate the visual pathway by detecting light.

Subject: Ophthalmology
QuestionText: What is the probable diagnosis for a 45-year-old man who arrives at the emergency ward with abrupt vision loss and experiencing painful eye movements? Upon examination, the affected eye shows a relative afferent pupillary defect and a normal optic disc. Additionally, visual field testing unveils a central scotoma in the same eye.
Explanation:
• The sudden loss of vision, painful eye movements, and relative afferent pupillary defects are
 suggestive of optic neuritis. The central scotoma on visual field testing further supports this diagnosis.

Subject: Ophthalmology
QuestionText: In a female patient, there is a complaint of vision loss, specifically affecting the right halves of both eyes. Where is the lesion located?
Explanation:
• The patient is presenting with loss of vision on the right side of both eyes, i.e., right-side homonymous
 hemianopia. So, the lesion will be on the left side.
 • Lesion at 3 sites of the visual pathway is responsible for right homonymous hemianopia: Left optic
 tract Left optic radiation Left visual cortex
 • Left optic tract
 • Left optic radiation
 • Left visual cortex
 • Among the given options, the correct answer is a lesion in the left optic tract, which would result in
 loss of vision in the right visual field of both eyes. This is because the fibers from the nasal side of the
 right eye retina cross at the optic chiasm and continue as the optic tract with temporal fibers from the
 left eye temporal retina.
 • Left optic tract
 • Left optic radiation
 • Left visual cortex

Subject: Ophthalmology
QuestionText: What would be the next investigative step for a 27-year-old female patient who presents with sudden vision loss accompanied by a relative afferent pupillary defect in her right eye, while her left eye appears normal on examination? MRI brain & orbit Myelin oligodendrocyte glycoprotein (MOG) and neuromyelitis optica (NMO) antibodies Blood sugar Thyroid profile
Explanation:
The sudden decrease in vision and relative afferent pupillary defect (RAPD) suggests a problem 
with the optic nerve in the right eye, most likely due to optic neuritis, which can be associated 
with diseases like multiple sclerosis (MS) or neuromyelitis optica (NMO).
 • MRI scan of the brain and orbit should preferably be performed in each case presenting with acute
 optic neuritis.
 • MOG and NMO antibodies: These antibodies are seen in autoimmune conditions affecting the central
 nervous system, like MS and NMO. Detection of these antibodies can help confirm the diagnosis.
 • Blood sugar levels have to be checked. Because Optic neuritis is Primarily treated with Steroids as
 Steroids may precipitate hyperglycemia in individuals with DM.
 On the other hand,
 4. Thyroid profile: A thyroid profile could be relevant in cases of thyroid eye disease, but this typically pr
 esents different symptoms, such as eye protrusion, redness, and double vision. Given the scenario, thy
 roid dysfunction is less likely.
 Thus, option a. is correct.

Subject: Ophthalmology
QuestionText: In a patient, a 33-year-old female presents with symptoms of gradual vision loss specifically affecting the right halves of both eyes. The question pertains to the location of the lesion in the optic pathway.
Explanation:
• In cases of progressive loss of vision in the right halves of both eyes, the most likely location of the
 lesion in the optic pathway is the left optic tract. The optic tracts carry visual information from the optic
 chiasm to the visual cortex in the brain. Lesions in the left optic tract can result in loss of vision in the
 right visual field of both eyes

Subject: Ophthalmology
QuestionText: The purpose of the photostress test is to distinguish between:
Explanation: Photostress Test helps in this differentiation:

Subject: Ophthalmology
QuestionText: What is the likely diagnosis for a 45-year-old man who arrives at the emergency department with sudden vision loss and painful eye movements? During examination, a relative afferent pupillary defect is observed in the affected eye, but the optic disk appears normal. Visual field testing shows a central scotoma in the same eye.
Explanation:
The above case is that of optic neuritis as there is sudden and painful loss of vision and there are no o
 ptic disc changes, RAPD or relative afferent pupillary defect and central scotoma is seen. Central scoto
 ma is the most common visual field defect seen.

Subject: Ophthalmology
QuestionText: Based on the image provided, what is the likely location of the lesion in a patient who is experiencing difficulty perceiving the lateral fields? The patient has been experiencing a mild headache for a few weeks and has now sought medical attention for further evaluation.
Explanation:
The key clinical relevance of the optic chiasm is related to the characteristic visual defects that occur w
 hen this structure is damaged or compressed - a condition commonly referred to as bitemporal hemian
opia, or 'tunnel vision.' In bitemporal hemianopia, the patient loses the outer (temporal) half of the field 
of vision in both eyes. This particular form of visual field loss is considered a classic sign of a lesion at t
 he level of the optic chiasm, as it directly results from the interruption of the crossing nasal fibers from 
each eye.
 To detect abnormalities in the optic chiasm, physicians typically use a
 combination of clinical examination techniques and imaging studies.
 Clinical examination involves assessing visual acuity, color vision, and particularly, the visual field. Visu
 al field testing, often performed using a technique called perimetry, is particularly helpful in identifying t
 he characteristic visual field defects associated with optic chiasm lesions.
 Imaging studies, specifically magnetic resonance imaging (MRI), are essential in visualizing the structu
 re of the optic chiasm and surrounding area. An MRI can reveal whether there's any compression or ot
 her pathology affecting the optic chiasm. Causes of optic chiasm compression may include brain tumor
 s (like pituitary adenomas), inflammation, or other lesions

Subject: Ophthalmology
QuestionText: Identify the site of the lesion.
Explanation:
• The left occipital lobe is part of the brain in the posterior region. It is primarily responsible for visual
 processing, interpreting color differentiation, and motion perception. Any condition affecting the left
 occipital lobe can disrupt these functions.
 • An impairment in the left occipital lobe, such as due to a trauma, stroke, or tumor, can lead to a
 specific type of visual field defect called right homonymous hemianopia. This is characterized by the
 loss of the right visual field in both eyes. It is vital to remember that the left occipital lobe processes
 visual information from the right visual field and vice versa, explaining why damage to the left lobe
 impacts the right visual field.
 • One can detect conditions affecting the left occipital lobe by looking out for the following symptoms

Subject: Ophthalmology
QuestionText: What is the diagnosis for a female patient, aged 45, who has a history of chickenpox and presents at the clinic with the following findings?
Explanation:
Based on the given information and findings, the most likely diagnosis is "Herpes zoster ophthalmicus."
 Explanation of the options:
 Option A: Chicken pox exacerbation: Chickenpox, caused by the varicella-zoster virus (VZV), typically 
presents with a generalized vesicular rash. The image provided does not show a
 generalized rash but rather a
 localized involvement of the ophthalmic division of the trigeminal nerve, suggesting a
 different diagnosis.
 Option B: Drug allergy to acyclovir: Acyclovir is an antiviral medication commonly used to treat herpes 
zoster (shingles) and other viral infections caused by herpesviruses. While drug allergies are possible, 
the presentation in the image is more consistent with the clinical features of a
 specific viral infection rather than a drug-related allergic reaction.
 Option C: Herpes zoster ophthalmicus: Herpes zoster ophthalmicus is a localized reactivation of the va
 ricella-zoster virus involving the ophthalmic division of the trigeminal nerve. It typically presents with ve
 sicular rash, erythema, and swelling in the distribution of the affected nerve, as seen in the image. The 
presence of vesicles in the distribution of the ophthalmic division of the trigeminal nerve, along with the 
patient's history of chickenpox, strongly suggests herpes zoster ophthalmicus.
 Option D: Herpes zoster oticus: Herpes zoster oticus, also known as Ramsay Hunt syndrome, is a spe
 cific form of herpes zoster that involves the facial nerve. It presents with vesicles and rash in the ear ca
 nal, earlobe, and sometimes on the face. The image provided does not show involvement of the ear or 
facial nerve distribution, making herpes zoster oticus less likely.
Therefore, based on the information and image provided, the most likely diagnosis is "Herpes zoster op
 hthalmicus."

Subject: Ophthalmology
QuestionText: In a patient diagnosed with a stroke, their visual field testing indicated macula sparing homonymous hemianopia. Where is the probable location of the lesion within the optic pathway?
Explanation:
• In the above given case, there is macula sparing homonymous hemianopia after a stroke which is
 indicative of the lesion being at the occipital lobe. There is loss of vision on the same side of the visual
 field in both eyes

Subject: Ophthalmology
QuestionText: A 60-year-old male was accompanied by his daughter to the outpatient department (OPD) due to his left eyelid drooping and experiencing double vision. Upon examination, it was observed that the pupil in the affected eye was dilated. The following eye movement examination was conducted. Which nerve is most likely injured in this case?
Explanation:
• In the above image, the left eye is down and out. There is ptosis present along with double vision and
 pupillary dilation. It is indicative of third nerve palsy or oculomotor nerve palsy

Subject: Ophthalmology
QuestionText: The following image indicates :
Explanation:
• The image indicates the presence of oculomotor nerve palsy. In the central position of gaze, the
 patient's left eye is observed to be deviated downward and outward, accompanied by drooping of the
 left eyelid (ptosis). Further examination of eye movements in different positions reveals the absence of
 inward movement (adduction) and upward movement (elevation) in the left eye, while downward
 movement (depression) is also reduced. These findings collectively suggest oculomotor nerve palsy

Subject: Ophthalmology
QuestionText: A 39-year-old woman arrives at the emergency department with a gradual loss of vision in her left eye over the past three months, as well as blurry vision in her right eye for the past 10 days. She has been experiencing frequent headaches for the past six months, which are worse in the morning and accompanied by nausea and vomiting. She has been taking ondansetron and paracetamol, which have provided some relief. Her visual acuity in her right eye is 20/30, while she can only count fingers with her left eye. An MRI scan reveals a frontal brain lesion on the left side. These findings suggest a diagnosis of Foster-Kennedy syndrome. Which of the following features is not typically seen in Foster-Kennedy syndrome?
Explanation:
•  Contralateral anosmia, contralateral central scotoma, bilateral progressive optic neuropathy. This
 option is incorrect because anosmia is not typically associated with Foster-Kennedy syndrome.
 Additionally, bilateral progressive optic neuropathy is not a characteristic feature of this syndrome.
 • Foster-Kennedy syndrome is a clinical syndrome characterized by a combination of optic atrophy in
 one eye and papilledema in the other eye. It is typically associated with a frontal brain lesion, such as a
 tumor or meningioma, which can cause compression or infiltration of the optic nerves and optic chiasm
OptionA: Ipsilateral optic atrophy, ipsilateral central scotoma, bilateral progressive optic neuropathy
OptionB: Contralateral papilledema, ipsilateral central scotoma, bilateral progressive optic neuropathy
OptionC: Ipsilateral optic atrophy, contralateral papilledema, ipsilateral central scotoma
OptionD: Contralateral anosmia, Contralateral central scotoma, bilateral progressive optic neuropathy

Subject: Ophthalmology
QuestionText: In the image, where is the location of the lesion that causes the visual field defect?
Explanation:
• The visual field defect shown in the image, specifically a 1/4th part of the ocular field defect(pie in the
 sky appearance), suggests a lesion in the temporal lobe. The temporal lobe plays a crucial role in
 processing visual information, particularly in the peripheral visual field. Lesions in the temporal lobe can
 result in specific visual field defects, such as a loss of vision in the corresponding quadrant of the visual
 field.

Subject: Ophthalmology
QuestionText: What type of field defects arise from a lesion located at the optic chiasma?
Explanation:
• A lesion at the optic chiasm, which is located at the base of the brain, can lead to a specific visual field
 defect called bitemporal hemianopia. In this condition, there is a loss of vision in the outer (temporal)
 half of the visual field of both eyes. This occurs because the optic nerve fibers carrying information from
 the nasal (inner) half of each retina cross over at the optic chiasm. Lesions at the optic chiasm, such as
 pituitary tumors, can compress or damage the crossing fibers, resulting in bitemporal hemianopia.

Subject: Ophthalmology
QuestionText: What is the correct information about the image provided below?
Explanation:
• Seen in optic neuritis, the pupil can paradoxically dilate in both eyes: This is the correct option. The
 swinging flashlight test is used to assess for an RAPD, which can be present in optic neuritis. In optic
 neuritis, when the affected eye is stimulated with light, the pupil can paradoxically dilate instead of
 constricting. This is due to the optic nerve dysfunction and altered signaling between the retina and the
 brain. The swinging flashlight test helps to detect this abnormal pupillary response
OptionA: It is called Argyl Robertson pupil
OptionC: The vision is normal in right eye
OptionD: Seen in optic neuritis, the pupil can paradoxically dilate in the both eyes

Subject: Neurology
QuestionText: Please identify the structure indicated in the image below.
Explanation: The marked structure in the image is the Pons, located just below the midbrain and above the medulla oblongata. It appears as a larger bulge in the brainstem on MRI or CT scans.

Subject: Neurology
QuestionText: The bleeding shown in the image is usually due to which of the following vessels?
Explanation: The bleeding is typically caused by the rupture of bridging veins, which are vulnerable in traumatic brain injuries and can lead to subdural hematomas.

Subject: Neurology
QuestionText: Please identify the cell that is indicated in the provided histological image...
Explanation: The marked cell is a Purkinje cell, characterized by a large soma and multiple branching dendrites, playing a crucial role in motor coordination.

Subject: Neurology
QuestionText: Which fibre is marked by the arrow in the image given below?
Explanation: The internal capsule contains projection fibers that transmit signals between the cerebral cortex and lower brain regions.

Subject: Neurology
QuestionText: Identify the structure marked B in the image given below:
Explanation: The fornix is a bundle of nerve fibers involved in memory and connects the hippocampus and mammillary bodies.

Subject: Neurology
QuestionText: Which of the following labels in this basal ganglia image is incorrect?
Explanation: The structure marked as 4 in the image is the external capsule, which is not the internal capsule.

Subject: Neurology
QuestionText: Identify the wrongly marked structure in the diagram.
Explanation: The wrongly marked structure is the pituitary gland.

Subject: Neurology
QuestionText: The structure indicated in the provided image originates from the...
Explanation: The fornix arises from the hippocampus.

Subject: Neurology
QuestionText: The nerve supply to the indicated muscle is derived from the following plexus.
Explanation: The area marked in the given image corresponds to the substantia nigra. The substantia nigra is a region located in the midbrain that plays a crucial role in the production and release of dopamine.

Subject: Neurology
QuestionText: A female patient presents to you with a unilateral headache. It is associated with nausea, photophobia, and phonophobia. What is the drug of choice for acute management?
Explanation: The patient's introduction suggests a migraine headache. Sumatriptan is a serotonin 5-HT1B/1D receptor agonist and is considered the drug of choice for acute administration of migraines. It works by constricting the cranial blood vessels and decreasing the release of inflammatory peptides, diminishing the headache and related symptoms.

Subject: Neurology
QuestionText: What is the most probable cause for the symptoms of seizures, along with a history of fever, headache, and confusion, in a male patient who arrived at the emergency room? An MRI brain scan revealed inflammation in the bitemporal lobe.
Explanation: The patient's headache, fever, confusion, and seizures indicate meningitis, with MRI showing inflammation of the temporal lobe, a pathognomic feature of Herpes Meningoencephalitis.

Subject: Neurology
QuestionText: A patient with a history of hypertension and taking multiple anti-hypertensive medications presents at the outpatient department (OPD). The provided ECG results indicate a particular drug as the cause for the observed ECG findings. Which of the following medications is accountable for these ECG findings?
Explanation: The ECG shows signs of hyperkalemia, common with potassium-sparing diuretics like spironolactone, which block aldosterone receptors and prevent K+ excretion.

Subject: Neurology
QuestionText: What is shown in given below image
Explanation: Port Wine Stain is a type of hemangioma usually present at birth, which fades with age but does not disappear.

Subject: Neurology
QuestionText: Lesion producing incongruous homonymous hemianopia with Wernicke’s pupil?
Explanation: Incongruous homonymous hemianopia refers to a visual field defect where there is loss of vision on one side of the visual field in both eyes, but the defect does not match exactly between the eyes. It is associated with Wernicke's pupil.

Subject: Neurology
QuestionText: What is the preferred medication for the presented case? A male patient, 54 years old, was brought to the emergency room with symptoms of fever, headache, and a focal seizure. The EEG results indicate the presence of spikes in the temporal lobe. Analysis of the cerebrospinal fluid (CSF) sample reveals the presence of 50 cells, with 90% lymphocytes and 10% neutrophils.
Explanation: Acyclovir is the drug of choice for viral meningitis indicated by CSF analysis with lymphocytic predominance and clinical symptoms suggestive of HSV meningitis.

Subject: Neurology
QuestionText: A patient comes with a blood pressure of 160/100 mmHg and left-sided facial paralysis and weakness since 8 hrs. Her CT appears normal. What would be your next step?
Explanation: This patient may be experiencing a transient ischemic attack or an ischemic stroke if they have sudden onset hemiparesis and a normal CT scan. For this patient, observation and monitoring would be the next course of action. The preferred imaging method for stroke patients is non-contrast CT (NCCT). For the first few hours, CT could appear normal, and the infarct might not be seen for 24-48 hours. The early detection of brain infarction is more easily detected using diffusion-weighted MRI (DW MRI).

Subject: Neurology
QuestionText: What is the probable diagnosis for a patient who presented at the emergency department with a complaint of intense headache, showing no specific neurological impairment except for the presence of neck stiffness, and subsequently underwent an urgent CT scan that revealed the following result?
Explanation: The above image is that of subarachnoid hemorrhage and shows blood in the basal cistern. Trauma is the most common cause. The patient describes it as the worst headache of their life, also known as a thunderclap headache. It presents with neck rigidity, transient loss of consciousness, and no focal neurological deficit.

Subject: Neurology
QuestionText: What should be the next course of action in the management of a 50-year-old male who experienced sudden onset of right-sided weakness and aphasia within a two-hour timeframe, with a recorded blood pressure of 160/110mmHg and a clear non-contrast computed tomography (NCCT) scan?
Explanation: The next step in management would be CT Angiography. NCCT and CT Angiography must be done concurrently during stroke evaluation. The given features of hemiparesis with aphasia and a clear NCCT are suggestive of an acute ischemic stroke with a proximal large vessel occlusion. Therefore, the patient should undergo immediate CT Angiography to localize.
OptionA: CT angiography to look for large vessel occlusion

Subject: Neurology
QuestionText: Which of the following statements regarding electroencephalogram (EEG) is not correct? (or) Which of the following statements regarding electroencephalogram (EEG) is correct?
Explanation: Doing EEG is not mandatory for the diagnosis of seizures. While EEG is valuable for characterizing seizures, diagnosis often relies on clinical history and examination. 1-5% of the population may have epileptiform discharges without clinical seizures. Scalp EEG can help localize frontal lobe epilepsy, but PML does not show specific triphasic patterns on EEG.
OptionA: 1-5% of the population can have epileptiform discharges
OptionB: Doing EEG is mandatory for diagnosis of seizures
OptionC: Scalp EEG may be helpful in localizing frontal lobe epilepsy
OptionD: Progressive multifocal leukoencephalopathy shows triphasic and slow waves on EEG

Subject: Neurology
QuestionText: In a patient who is 66 years old and has a history of chronic hypertension, he arrives at the emergency room with changes in mental state and high blood pressure measuring 210/110 mm Hg. A brain CT scan shows bleeding within the brain tissue. The typical location of this bleeding in similar cases is known as the:
Explanation: In chronic hypertension, the most common site of intraparenchymal hemorrhage is the Putamen. Chronic hypertension causes vessel rupture due to weakened small blood vessels, often resulting in hemorrhage in the basal ganglia.

Subject: Neurology
QuestionText: A 28-year-old male was brought to the emergency room with a history of RTA. GCS progressively declines over hours and axial T2 MRI below shows multiple small hypointense lesions at the corticomedullary junction and basal ganglia, what is the diagnosis?
Explanation: The most likely diagnosis is diffuse axonal injury (DAI) due to acceleration-deceleration forces during a motor vehicle accident. DAI is characterized by widespread axonal injury and a progressive decline in GCS, with MRI showing hypointense lesions at the corticomedullary junction and basal ganglia.

Subject: Neurology
QuestionText: In a patient undergoing visual field testing, the observed loss suggests a potential site of lesion within the optic pathway.
Explanation: The occipital lobe is responsible for processing visual information. A lesion in this lobe can cause visual disturbances like field defects or hallucinations, consistent with the question scenario.

Subject: Neurology
QuestionText: A 16-year-old girl, who is taking antiepileptics, has had a seizure-free period of 6 months. She has no family history of epilepsy. Her EEG is now normal and she has a normal neurological exam and intelligence. What would your advice be?
Explanation:
Continue treatment for another 2 years: In this scenario, the patient has experienced a seizure-free
period of 6 months and has a normal EEG, normal neurological examination, and normal intelligence.
However, it is recommended to continue treatment with antiepileptic drugs (AEDs) for another 2 years
before considering the option of tapering or discontinuing the medication. This is because the risk of
seizure recurrence after a seizure-free period is highest within the first 2 years. Continuing treatment for
this duration helps to consolidate the remission and reduce the risk of relapse.
OptionA: Stop the treatment and follow up
OptionB: Gradually taper the drug and stop treatment

Subject: Neurology
QuestionText: A 16 year old girl, who is taking antiepileptics, has had a seizure-free period of 6 months. She has no family history of epilepsy. Her EEG is now normal and she has a normal neurological exam and intelligence. What would be your advice?
Explanation: The recommended approach is to continue treatment for another 2 years. Generally, 2-5 years of seizure freedom is considered necessary before considering discontinuation of antiepileptic drugs.
OptionA: Stop the treatment and follow up
OptionB: Gradually taper the drug and stop treatment

QuestionText: Identify the diagnosis?
Explanation: Plexiform neurofibromatosis is a subtype of neurofibromatosis type 1 (NF1) and involves the development of benign tumors (neurofibromas) that can cause disfigurement and functional impairments, arising from multiple nerves.

Subject: Neurology
QuestionText: The given pattern of EEG is found in:
Explanation: Creutzfeldt-Jakob disease (CJD) is characterized by specific EEG findings such as periodic sharp wave complexes (PSWCs) and generalized slowing, consistent with the given EEG pattern.

Subject: Neurology
QuestionText: What is the probable diagnosis for a 72-year-old former pilot who visits a neurologist, accompanied by his wife, exhibiting symptoms of rapidly advancing dementia, myoclonus, ataxia, and visual impairments? The provided FLAIR MRI scans can assist in confirming the diagnosis.
Explanation: Variant CJD is characterized by rapidly progressive dementia, myoclonus, ataxia, and visual disturbances. The hyperintense signal seen in FLAIR MRI scans, known as the hockey stick sign, is diagnostic for this condition.

Subject: Neurology
QuestionText: What is the probable diagnosis for a patient who exhibits symptoms of increased urination, nighttime urination, and involuntary urination, along with a recorded 24-hour urine volume of 7 liters and a urine osmolarity of 260 mOsm/L? The ADH assay shows a recorded value of 0.8 pg/ml, and an MRI of the brain reveals the absence of a bright spot on T1 weighted imaging.
Explanation: This case presents symptoms of polyuria and polydipsia, which are classic for diabetes insipidus. The absence of a bright spot on imaging supports the diagnosis of central diabetes insipidus.

Subject: Neurology
QuestionText: A patient was asked to copy the face of a clock and his drawing is shown below. What is the correct term to be used here?
Explanation: Hemispatial neglect refers to the condition where a patient is unable to notice or respond to stimuli on one side of space. The clock drawn by the patient, which is blank on one side, exemplifies this condition. It indicates that while the patient has the ability to perform the task, they fail to acknowledge one half of the visual field.

Subject: Neurology
QuestionText: What is the most suitable medication for treating a female patient with galactorrhea, who has tested negative for pregnancy and has been diagnosed with a significant pituitary tumor through an MRI, considering her refusal for surgery?
Explanation: Bromocriptine effectively reduces prolactin levels and alleviates symptoms of hyperprolactinemia, making it the preferred treatment for prolactinomas.

Subject: Neurology
QuestionText: Unilateral pulsatile headache in a woman with photophobia and phonophobia which is exaggerated on eating meals is seen in?
Explanation: Migraines are characterized by unilateral, pulsatile headaches that may be accompanied by nausea and sensitivity to light and sound. The mnemonic POUND helps recall the symptoms, reinforcing migraine as the diagnosis in the context described.

Subject: Neurology
QuestionText: In the provided image, the patient's neuromuscular monitoring following anesthesia is depicted. Which nerve is most frequently utilized for this purpose?
Explanation: The ulnar nerve is commonly used for neuromuscular monitoring due to its accessibility and sensitivity to neuromuscular blockers. It innervates the adductor pollicis muscle, allowing anesthesiologists to gauge neuromuscular blockade effectively.

Subject: Neurology
QuestionText: What should be the subsequent course of action for an elderly patient who has undergone full removal of glioblastoma?
Explanation: → Glioblastoma is a type of brain tumor that arises from astrocytes, the supportive cells in the brain. The treatment of glioblastoma usually involves surgery, radiation therapy, and chemotherapy. After surgical excision of the glioblastoma, the next step in management depends on several factors such as the extent of the surgery, the age and overall health of the patient, and the tumor's aggressiveness. → Radiotherapy is the next step in management in this case after complete excision of glioblastoma. It involves the use of high-energy X-rays or other types of radiation to kill the remaining cancer cells and prevent the tumor from recurring. Radiotherapy is usually given over several weeks and may be accompanied by chemotherapy. Incorrect Choices: → Option a. Chemotherapy with Bevacizumab is an option for recurrent glioblastoma or for patients who are not able to undergo radiation therapy due to various reasons. It may also be used as an adjuvant therapy in combination with radiation therapy and temozolomide. However, it is not the next step in the management of an old patient who has undergone complete excision of glioblastoma. → Option b. No further management is not an appropriate option for glioblastoma patients who have undergone complete excision of the tumor. These patients require additional treatment to prevent the tumor from recurring. → Option c. Chemoradiation is usually the next step in management after surgery for patients with glioblastoma. It involves the use of chemotherapy and radiation therapy in combination to treat the remaining cancer cells and prevent recurrence. However, this option is usually reserved for patients with residual tumor after surgery.

Subject: Neurology
QuestionText: What is the diagnosis of a patient, aged 50, who had a trauma one week ago and now presents with confusion and ataxia? The patient has a history of alcohol dependence and has not consumed alcohol for the past two weeks. The CT scan shows a specific finding.
Explanation: → The patient's presentation of confusion and ataxia, coupled with a history of trauma and the given CT scan findings, points towards a subdural hemorrhage, making option b the correct answer. Subdural hematomas are usually due to a tear in the bridging veins that run between the dura mater and the brain surface. Incorrect Choices: → Option a. Extradural hemorrhage: This would typically present acutely after a head injury and show a lens-shaped (or biconvex) hematoma on a CT scan. It is due to a rupture of the middle meningeal artery usually. → Option c. Intra-parenchymal hemorrhage: This would result in bleeding within the brain tissue itself, often associated with hypertension, cerebral amyloid angiopathy, or a traumatic brain injury. → Option d. Subarachnoid hemorrhage: This would typically present with a "thunderclap" headache and is due to bleeding into the subarachnoid space, often from a ruptured aneurysm.

Subject: Neurology
QuestionText: What is the most appropriate course of action for the management of a patient who presents to the emergency department with a severe headache, and has a blood pressure reading of 160/100 mmHg, along with a CT scan showing evidence of a subarachnoid hemorrhage?
Explanation: Option B → The correct answer is Angiography. 1608 → Following a confirmed diagnosis of subarachnoid hemorrhage (SAH) via CT scan, the most appropriate next step is to perform a cerebral angiography. → This diagnostic procedure provides a detailed visualization of the blood vessels in the brain and helps to determine the cause of the SAH, which is often a ruptured aneurysm. Incorrect Choices: → Option A. Nimodipine is a calcium channel blocker often used in SAH management to prevent vasospasm, a common complication of SAH. However, it is not typically the immediate next step following diagnosis. → Option C. Surgery may be required to treat the cause of the SAH, such as a ruptured aneurysm. However, angiography is typically performed first to confirm the location and extent of the aneurysm. → Option D. Fibrinolytic therapy is not typically used in the acute management of SAH. It dissolves dangerous intravascular clots to prevent ischemic damage by improving blood flow.

Subject: Neurology
QuestionText: A 34-year-old woman was brought to the AIIMS emergency following a road traffic accident. She was hemodynamically stable and her GCS score was 3. She was intubated and sent for non-contrast CT which was found to be normal. What is the most likely diagnosis?
Explanation: Correct Option C - Diffuse axonal injury: → In a patient who presents following a road traffic accident with a low GCS score, the most likely diagnosis is diffuse axonal injury (DAI). → Diffuse axonal injury (DAI) is one of the most common and important pathologic features of traumatic brain injury (TBI) which occurs due to widespread shearing and stretching of axonal fibers in the brain, resulting from rapid acceleration and deceleration forces during the accident. → It often occurs without any visible abnormalities on imaging studies such as CT scans.

Subject: Neurology
QuestionText: What is the probable diagnosis for a 15-year-old boy who has a painless, gradually enlarging, soft, and elastic swelling on the lateral aspect of his neck, as depicted in the provided image? Additionally, the examination reveals the presence of freckles and skin patches on his face and trunk.
Explanation: Correct Option C: → Based on the given information, the most likely diagnosis is a neural tumor. Neural tumors arise from the cells of the nervous system and can present as slow-growing, rubbery, non-tender swellings. The presence of freckles and skin patches over the face and trunk further supports the suspicion of a neural tumor. → Neural tumors can include various types, such as neurofibromas, schwannomas, or ganglioneuromas. These tumors can occur in different locations along the peripheral nerves, including the neck.

Subject: ENT
QuestionText: What is the term for the view that provides the clearest visualization of the frontal sinus?
Explanation:
The position that allows for the clearest visualization of the frontal sinus is known as the "Caldwell's vie
 w." The patient's head is tilted as an X-ray of the skull is taken, and the X-ray beam is directed through 
the frontal sinuses and nasal bones. This method is widely used to examine the frontal sinuses and fin
 d any anatomical abnormalities or diseases, such as sinusitis or fractures. Other X-ray projections are 
used to see different parts of the skull, including Towne's view, Law's view, and Stenvers view.
 Therefore, the correct view is Caldwell's view.
 Caldwell view (Occipitofrontal view or nose-forehead position). The view is taken with the nose and for
 ehead touching the film and X-ray beam is projected 15–20° caudally. Structures seen are: (a) Frontal 
sinuses (seen best). (b) Ethmoid sinuses. (c) Maxillary sinuses. (d) Frontal process of zygoma and zyg
 omatic process of frontal bone. (e) Superior margin of orbit and lamina papyracea. (f) Superior orbital fi
 ssure. (g) Foramen rotundum (inferolateral to superior orbital fissure).

Subject: ENT
QuestionText: What would be your diagnosis for a patient who has a prior history of trauma and is currently experiencing symptoms such as double vision, nosebleeds, and limited eye movements, based on the provided CT image?
Explanation:
The most likely diagnosis, based on the supplied CT imaging, is a blowout fracture. A blowout fracture 
can cause symptoms including diplopia (double vision), epistaxis (nosebleed), and limited eye moveme
 nt and is often caused by blunt force trauma to the orbit (eye socket).
 When the thin orbital bones—particularly the floor—are broken, it results in a blowout fracture, which c
 auses the eye and other contents of the orbit to herniate or drop into the maxillary sinus below. This m
 ay result in the extraocular muscles becoming trapped, limiting eye movement, and causing diplopia. T
 herefore, the correct option is a blow-out fracture.

Explanation:
The maxillary sinuses are the largest of the paranasal sinuses. 
• They are located within the maxillary bones of the skull,
 • Because of their location, the maxillary sinuses are susceptible to infection or inflammation, which can
 cause symptoms such as pain or pressure in the cheeks or upper teeth, congestion, headache, or
 fever.

QuestionText: X-ray beam angled at 45° to the orbitomeatal line is also known as?
Explanation:

QuestionText: What is the commonly used name for the view shown in the radiograph below, which is used to assess the frontal sinus more effectively?
Explanation:
• The radiograph shown in the question is the Caldwell view, also known as the Caldwell projection or
 Caldwell radiograph. It is a radiographic technique used to obtain a better assessment of the frontal
 sinus and the anterior ethmoidal cells.
 • In the Caldwell view, the X-ray beam is directed from below the patient, angled upward toward the

Subject: ENT
QuestionText: Please identify the structure indicated in the endoscopic image of the right nasal cavity.
Explanation:
• In the endoscopic view of the right nasal cavity, the marked structure corresponds to the inferior
 turbinate. The inferior turbinate, also known as the inferior nasal concha, is a bony structure covered
 with respiratory mucosa located on the lateral wall of the nasal cavity.
 • The inferior turbinate plays a crucial role in the nasal airflow and conditioning of inhaled air. It helps to
 humidify, filter, and warm the inspired air as it passes through the nasal cavity. The surface of the
 inferior turbinate is covered with ciliated epithelium and numerous blood vessels, which help in the air
 conditioning process.

Subject: ENT
QuestionText: What is the probable diagnosis based on the finding given in the CT image?
Explanation:
• Pneumatization of the superior turbinate refers to the presence of an air cell within the superior
 turbinate, which is one of the nasal structures involved in regulating airflow and humidification. Similar
 to concha bullosa, pneumatized superior turbinate is typically an incidental finding and does not
 typically causes vertigo, tinnitus, and head tilt.

QuestionText: A 23-year-old male patient visits you reporting a nasal discharge that appears like the image shown on a handkerchief. Which of the following characteristics aids in identifying the condition as CSF rhinorrhoea?

 • It is also known as the double target sign or the target sign which is seen in CSF rhinorrhoea and is

 adenoid hypertrophy.

Explanation:

QuestionText: What is the term used to describe the sign depicted in the contrast-enhanced CT scan of the head shown below in a 14-year-old football player who presented to the casualty with complaints of epistaxis while playing football, along with a history of recurrent spontaneous nasal bleeding, unilateral nasal obstruction, and denasal speech, without any history of trauma or falls?

QuestionText: What is the probable diagnosis for a 30-year-old male who has nonaxial proptosis in his left eye and a history of a road traffic accident 15 years ago, as suggested by the provided CT image? (or) What is the diagnosis of the below CT image with history of nonaxial proptosis of the left eye?

• This commonly occurs as a result of obstruction to the frontal ostium due to chronic disease of the

 Vision changes or double vision Nasal congestion or discharge Sinus pressure or pain Swelling or

Subject: ENT
QuestionText: A gentleman presents to the OPD with a saddle nose deformity. He gives a history of cough, on-and-off fever, and hemoptysis. On examination, septal perforation is present with pale granuloma. His chest X-ray showed multiple cavitary lesions, and the biopsy showed granuloma, multinucleated giant cells, and caseous necrosis. What is the probable cause?
Explanation:
Nasal tuberculosis can occur secondary to pulmonary tuberculosis.
 • Commonly involves the anterior end of the inferior turbinate and the anterior part of the nasal septum.
 • Chronic inflammation can destroy the nasal septum resulting in a deformity called saddle nose
 deformity.
 • It is diagnosed by biopsy, which shows caseating granulomas, Langhans multinucleated giant cells,
 and necrotic debris.

Subject: ENT
QuestionText: A South Indian male farmer presents to the ENT OPD with complaints of reddish mass coming out from the nose and histopathology image as given below. What is the likely diagnosis? (or) What is the likely diagnosis based on the images given below?
Explanation:
• Rhinosporidiosis is a chronic fungal infection caused by the organism Rhinosporidium seeberi. It
 primarily affects the mucous membranes of the nose and occasionally other sites like the eye, skin, and
 genitals.
 • Rhinosporidiosis is commonly seen in South India.
 • The clinical presentation of rhinosporidiosis often includes the presence of reddish or pinkish masses
 or polyps in the nasal cavity.
 • Histopathology shows sporangium with sporangiospores.
 • These masses can vary in size and may bleed when touched or traumatized. The given image
 showing a mass protruding from the nose is suggestive of this characteristic presentation.

Subject: ENT
QuestionText: A 65-year-old patient presents with right-sided nasal obstruction and occasional blood-stained nasal discharge. His CT and endoscopy findings are shown in the below image. What could be the underlying cause? (or) CT and endoscopy findings are shown in the below image. What could be the underlying cause?
Explanation:
• The image shows Inverted papilloma causing erosion or remodeling of adjacent bony structures, such
 as the nasal septum or the walls of the sinuses.
 • Inverted papilloma is a benign sinonasal tumor that arises from the lining of the nasal cavity or
 paranasal sinuses.
 • It often presents with unilateral nasal obstruction, recurrent nosebleeds, and the potential for locally
 aggressive behavior

QuestionText: What is the accurate statement about the condition depicted in the image, as presented by a child?
Explanation: → Kwashiorkor is a type of malnutrition characterized by severe protein deficiency. → It causes fluid retention and a swollen, distended abdomen. → Because of edema, children with kwashiorkor may look healthy, so their parents view them as well-fed. Incorrect Choices: → Option b. Kwashiorkor is a type of moderate to severe malnutrition due to severe protein deficiency. It is not because of calorie deficiency. → Option c. Marasmus is a type of moderate to severe malnutrition due to rapid deterioration in nutritional status mainly caused by a deficiency in calories and energy. → Option d. There is no edema.

Subject: Pediatrics
QuestionText: As per the current suggested dietary guidelines for children, the proportion of total energy intake derived from saturated fats should be _______.
Explanation: Correct Option A: → < 10%: This is the correct choice. According to the current recommended dietary guidelines for children, the intake of saturated fats should be less than 10% of total energy intake. This guideline is in place to promote a diet that is low in saturated fats and encourages the consumption of healthier sources of fats, such as unsaturated fats. Incorrect choices : Option B. < 5%: This choice suggests that saturated fats should constitute less than 5% of total energy intake. However, this is not the current recommendation for children. While it is true that limiting satura ted fat intake is beneficial, the recommended threshold for children is slightly higher than 5%. Option C. < 15%: This choice suggests that saturated fats should be less than 15% of total energy inta ke. Although a limit of 15% may be applicable for adults or certain age groups, it is not the recommend ed guideline specifically for children. Option D. < 30%: This choice suggests that saturated fats should be less than 30% of total energy inta ke. However, this figure is too high for the recommended intake of saturated fats in children. High cons umption of saturated fats is associated with an increased risk of cardiovascular diseases, and the guid elines aim to limit their intake.

Subject: Pediatrics
QuestionText: What is the diagnosis for a 7-month-old child presented by her mother with complaints of diarrhea and mucocutaneous lesions, as depicted in the image? These symptoms have been observed since the initiation of weaning.
Explanation: Correct Option: B → Based on the information provided, the most likely diagnosis for the child's symptoms after weaning from breast milk is Acrodermatitis enteropathica. → Acrodermatitis enteropathica is a rare autosomal recessive disorder characterized by impaired zinc absorption, leading to zinc deficiency. It typically manifests when breast milk or formula is replaced with a zinc-deficient diet, such as solid foods. The condition is characterized by diarrhea, skin lesions, and other mucocutaneous manifestations. The key features of Acrodermatitis enteropathica include: The other options listed are less likely based on the given information: Option A: Wiskott Aldrich syndrome is a primary immunodeficiency disorder characterized by recurrent infections, eczema, thrombocytopenia (low platelet count), and small platelet size. It typically presents i n early childhood and is not specifically associated with weaning or mucocutaneous lesions. Option C: Pediatric epidermolysis bullosa is a group of inherited disorders characterized by fragile skin and mucous membranes, leading to blistering and erosions with minimal trauma. It can be present at bi rth or develop in early childhood, but the association with weaning and diarrhea is less typical. Option D: Atopic dermatitis is a chronic inflammatory skin condition characterized by dry, itchy skin and a rash. It often starts in infancy or early childhood and can be associated with a personal or family history of allergies. While it can present with diarrhea, the mucocutaneous lesions described in the question a re not specific for atopic dermatitis.

Subject: Pediatrics
QuestionText: A child comes to your outpatient department exhibiting the findings depicted in the image provided. What is the probable diagnosis?
Explanation: Correct choice : A Explanation Kwashiorkor It is a form of malnutrition caused by a lack of protein in the diet. It causes fluid retention and a swollen, distended abdomen. Kwashiorkor mo st commonly affects children, particularly in developing countries with high levels of poverty and food in security. Wellcome classification is a system for classifying proteinnenergy malnutrition in children based on per centage of expected weightnfornage and the presence or absence of oedema. Wellcome Trust Classification Weight for Age (Boston) (% of expected) Odema Clinical Type of PSM 60-80 + Kwashiorkor - Underweight <60 Marasmus Marasmic Kwashiorkor Incorrect choices Option B: Marasmus is an undernourishment causing a child's weight to be significantly low for their age. It occurs as a result of total calorie insufficiency. This leads to overt loss of adipose tissue and muscle. Option C: Nephrotic syndrome is a kidney disorder that causes the body to excrete too much protein in the urine. Kidney disease that affects a kidney's filtering system is the most common cause of nephroti c syndrome in children. The type of nephrotic syndrome that is most common in children is called idiop athic nephrotic syndrome. Option D: Nephritic syndrome is a clinical syndrome that presents as hematuria, elevated blood pressu re, decreased urine output, and edema. In children, the most common cause of acute glomerulonephrit is is post-streptococcal glomerulonephritis which leads to sudden onset of the nephritic syndrome in se ven to ten days after a streptococcal throat or 2-3 weeks after a skin infection (impetigo).

Subject: Ophthalmology
QuestionText: Which investigation can be harmful to this patient with a suspected foreign body in his eye, who was admitted to the hospital following an eye injury incurred while using a hammer and iron chisel to cut stone in an industrial setting?
Explanation:
• An MRI uses a strong magnetic field and radio waves to generate detailed images of the body's
 structures. However, it is contraindicated in cases where there is a risk of metallic foreign bodies, such
 as in this industrial worker with a suspected foreign body in the eye. The strong magnetic field of an
 MRI can cause movement or displacement of the metallic object, potentially leading to further injury or
 damage to the eye

Subject: Ophthalmology
QuestionText: Within 6 weeks of experiencing a penetrating accidental injury to his left eye caused by a wooden stick, a 28-year-old man presented with symptoms including severe vision loss, pain, redness, and sensitivity to light in his right eye. QuestionText: What is the most probable diagnosis for a 40-year-old male patient who has a swelling that has been gradually increasing in size over the last two years, with a variable consistency and complete mobility upon examination?

QuestionText: Please provide the relevant information and context for the diagnosis of this 60-year-old man.
Explanation: Correct Option D: → The diagnosis for this 60-year-old man is SCC (Squamous Cell Carcinoma) of the lip, as indicated by the provided image. → Based on the image showing SCC of the lip, it is evident that the patient has a malignant tumor involving the lip. Squamous cell carcinoma is a type of skin cancer that commonly occurs on sun-exposed areas, including the lips. It typically presents as a non-healing ulcer or a raised, crusted lesion with irregular borders. Incorrect Option: Option A: Basal cell carcinoma is another common type of skin cancer, but it typically presents with diff erent clinical features and is less likely to be associated with lip involvement.
