Subject: Ophthalmology
QuestionText: What is the likely reason for a 21-year-old student to have ocular redness, feeling of a foreign object in the eye, and excessive tearing after wearing a contact lens, as indicated by the corneal scraping interface contrast microscopy image?
Explanation:
• The interface contrast microscopy image shows cysts of Acanthamoeba, a free-living protozoan that
 can cause a serious corneal infection known as Acanthamoeba keratitis (AK). AK is a potential
 complication of contact lens use and can cause symptoms such as ocular redness, foreign body
 sensation, excessive tearing, and blurred vision. In severe cases, AK can lead to corneal scarring and
 vision loss.

Subject: Ophthalmology
QuestionText: Which refractive error is related to the provided image among the following options?
Explanation:
• Astigmatism is a refractive error that results from an irregularly shaped cornea or lens in the eye,
 which causes blurred or distorted vision. It can cause difficulties with both near and distance vision. In
 astigmatism, the cornea or lens has different curvatures in different meridians, which leads to a focus of
 light at two different points instead of a single point. This results in distorted images, which can be seen
 in the image provided

Subject: Ophthalmology
QuestionText: What is the term used to describe the refractive error seen in the ocular examination image of a 60-year-old patient who has complained of reduced distant vision, but no longer requires spectacles for near vision?
Explanation:
• The type of refractive error seen in the given ocular examination image of a 60-year-old patient who
 complains of decreased distant vision but no longer uses glasses for near vision is called index myopia.
 • The given image is of Nuclear cataract causing index myopia
 • Index myopia, also known as refractive myopia, is a type of myopia that is caused by a refractive error
 in the eye's lens rather than a structural anomaly such as elongation of the eyeball (axial myopia) or
 changes in the cornea's curvature (curvatural myopia).
 • This refractive error occurs when the refractive index of the lens changes, causing the light entering
 the eye to converge in front of the retina instead of on it, resulting in blurred distance vision.

Subject: Ophthalmology
QuestionText: During a routine eye examination, a patient's visual acuity was evaluated using Snellen's chart, and it was determined to be 6/6. What is the smallest angle at which this patient can differentiate two separate points or objects?
Explanation:
The minimum angle of resolution with 6/6 vision is 5 minutes of arc
 Visual acuity is essentially a
 measure of the eye's ability to distinguish object details and shape, evaluated from a
 set distance. For the question at hand, Snellen’s chart was used for this purpose.
 • Snellen’s Chart and 6/6 Vision: The Snellen’s chart is a standard tool used worldwide to evaluate
 visual acuity. When an individual records a vision of 6/6, it means they can see clearly at 6 meters what
 should normally be seen at that distance.
 • Minimum Angle of Resolution (MAR): The MAR, linked to visual acuity, is the smallest visual angle a
 person can perceive. It’s the angle subtended by the details of an object that are just discernible. The
 normal MAR for human vision is 1 minute of arc, which is equivalent to 1/60th of a degree.
 • 6/6 Vision and MAR: A vision of 6/6 correlates to a MAR of 1 minute of arc. But, in practicality, we
 consider it to be about 5 minutes of arc for identifying letters or numbers on the Snellen chart.

Subject: Ophthalmology
QuestionText: In myopia image is formed at
Explanation:
Myopia, also known as nearsightedness, is a refractive error of the eye where the eyeball is longer tha
 n normal or the cornea has excessive curvature. This causes light rays to focus in front of the retina ins
 tead of directly on it.

Subject: Ophthalmology
QuestionText: What is the likely diagnosis for a 5-year-old girl who came for her first comprehensive eye examination after a school vision screening revealed unequal visual acuity? The patient's parents haven't observed any eye issues. The right eye has a visual acuity of 20/20 while the left eye has a visual acuity of 20/80. The rest of the examination, including the Fundus exam, showed no abnormalities.
Explanation:
The above is a case of amblyopia. In amblyopia, there is an uncorrectable decrease in vision in one or 
both eyes with no apparent structural abnormality seen to explain it. A
 difference of two or more lines on the visual acuity chart between the two eyes or a
 best corrected vision of 20/20 vision of 20/30 or worse is amblyopia

Subject: Ophthalmology
QuestionText: Identify the instrument.
Explanation:
• The instrument shown in the image is a Maddox rod. It is a diagnostic tool used in ophthalmology to
 assess eye alignment and evaluate for ocular deviations or muscle imbalances. The Maddox rod
 consists of a cylindrical lens with parallel lines running along its length.
 • The Maddox rod is typically used in the evaluation of horizontal strabismus (ocular misalignment) and
 is particularly useful in detecting and measuring the presence of a phoria (latent misalignment) or
 atropia (manifest misalignment).
 • When a patient looks through the Maddox rod, the parallel lines cause a displacement of light,
 creating a streak or line of light in front of one eye

Subject: Ophthalmology
QuestionText: At a distance of 60m, what is the measurement of the angle formed by the largest letter on the Snellen's chart?
Explanation:
The angle subtended by the largest alphabet on the Snellen's chart at a 60-meter distance is 5
 minutes of arc.
 • The Snellen's chart is a commonly used tool for measuring visual acuity. The chart consists of several
 rows of letters, with each row representing a certain level of visual acuity. The largest letter on the
 Snellen's chart, typically located at the top, is designed to be seen at a distance of 6 meters (20 feet) by
 individuals with normal visual acuity.
 • To determine the angle subtended by an object, we can use the formula:
 • Angle (in minutes of arc) = (Size of the object / Distance to the object) * 3438
 • In this case, the distance to the object (the Snellen's chart) is 60 meters. Since the largest letter on the
 chart is designed to be seen at a distance of 6 meters, we can calculate the size of the letter at the
 given distance using the formula:
 • Size of the letter = (Size of the letter at 6 meters / Distance to the object) * 60
 • Assuming the size of the largest letter on the chart at 6 meters is 5 minutes of arc, we can substitute
 the values into the formula:
 • Size of the letter = (5 / 6) * 60 = 50 minutes of arc
 • Therefore, the angle subtended by the largest alphabet on the Snellen's chart at a 60-meter distance
 is 50 minutes of arc, which corresponds to the correct answer choice of A.

Subject: Ophthalmology
QuestionText: Which of the following conditions is not managed using the spectacles depicted in the image? (or) Which of the following conditions is not managed using the spectacles depicted in the image?
Explanation:
• Pediatric pseudophakia refers to the implantation of an intraocular lens (IOL) in the eye of a child who
 has had their natural lens removed, usually due to a cataract or other congenital eye conditions. In this
 case, spectacles are not used for vision correction because an IOL is implanted to replace the natural
 lens. Spectacles may be used for certain specific purposes like near vision, but the primary correction
 for vision comes from the intraocular lens.

Subject: Ophthalmology
QuestionText: What is the purpose of the test shown in the enclosed image?
Explanation:
• The test shown in the image, the Titmus stereotest, is used for assessing stereopsis, which is the
 ability to perceive depth and three-dimensional vision. Therefore, the correct answer is B: Stereopsis
 test.
 • Stereopsis test: The Titmus stereotest is a commonly used test to assess stereopsis and depth
 perception. It uses various stereoscopic images to determine a person's ability to perceive depth and
 identify disparities between the two eyes.

Subject: Ophthalmology
QuestionText: In the provided image, which nerve is subjected to blockage?
Explanation:
• The nasociliary nerve courses along the medial wall of the orbit.
 • It branches into the posterior and anterior ethmoidal nerves and the infratrochlear nerve. It innervates
 the lacrimal sac, inner canthus, and lateral aspect of the nose.
 • To block the infratrochlear nerve (a branch of the nasociliary nerve), the needle is inserted just above
 the medial canthal ligament along the medial wall of the orbit to a depth of 1.0 cm. For procedures like
 dacryocystorhinostomy, the anterior ethmoidal nerve (also a branch of the nasociliary nerve) with its
 distal external nasal branch is blocked using the same procedure but to a depth of 2.0 cm posterior to
 the anterior lacrimal crest.
 • Blocking the nasociliary nerve, particularly its branches, leads to anesthesia in the innervated areas,
 providing pain relief during procedures involving the lacrimal sac, inner canthus, and lateral aspect of
 the nose.

Subject: Ophthalmology
QuestionText: Please identify the medical condition depicted in the provided image.
Explanation:
The above image is of Entropion. Entropion is the inward rolling of the eye margin towards the globe w
 hich is seen here. The most common cause being old age.
 · Spastic entropion: There is long-term patching which causes spasm of the muscle
 Treatment: Use botulinum toxin
 Treat the cause of spasm
 Surgical intervention: Displaced fibers of orbicularis oculi cause the eyelashes of the lower lid to turn 
inwards so surgery is needed to correct the laxity of the lower eyelid

Subject: Ophthalmology
QuestionText: What would be your diagnosis for a patient who has a previous trauma history and is currently experiencing symptoms such as diplopia, epistaxis, and limited eye movements, upon examination of the provided CT image?
Explanation:
Blowout Fracture: A
 blowout fracture typically occurs when there is blunt trauma to the orbit (eye socket), resulting in a fract
 ure of the thin bones surrounding the orbit. This type of fracture often leads to the herniation of orbital c
 ontents, causing symptoms such as diplopia, epistaxis, and restricted eye movements. The CT image 
given above also supports this diagnosis.

Subject: Ophthalmology
QuestionText: What is the probable diagnosis for a man of 45 years who arrives at the emergency department with an abrupt loss of vision and experiencing painful movements of the eye? Upon examination, a relative afferent pupillary defect is observed in the affected eye, but the optic disk appears normal. Further visual field testing demonstrates a central scotoma in the same eye.
Explanation:
• Summary: 45 year old man, sudden loss of vision and painful ocular movements, relative afferent
 pupillary defect (RAPD) in the affected eye, central scotoma seen in the same eye.
 • Most likely diagnosis: Optic neuritis
• Confirmation of the diagnosis: By the perimetry - in case of optic neuritis, either we get the central
 scotoma or the centrocecal scotoma (‘cecal’ means involving the blind spot).
 • Next step: Treatment is guided by the degree of the patient’s visual impairment, visual needs,
 presence of previous episodes, results of MRI and specific etiology, if identified. General guidelines for
 treatment are based on major multicentral trial (the Optic Neuritis Treatment Trial [ONTT]).

Subject: Ophthalmology
QuestionText: A male patient, aged 56, comes to the doctor with concerns about his eyes bulging and becoming more prominent over the past month. He has previously been diagnosed with Grave's disease. Apart from this, he is in good overall health. A physical examination reveals a swelling in the front of his neck that moves upward when he swallows, but does not move when he sticks out his tongue. The image below shows the patient's eyes. Based on these findings, what is the most probable diagnosis for the patient's current symptoms?
Explanation:
Thyroid eye disease
 • This is a case of thyroid eye disease in which the patient presents with complaints of progressive
 bilateral prominence and bulging of eyes for 1 week.
 • The patient is a known case of Grave's disease, in which there is the possibility of having thyroid eye
 disease.
 • On examination, the patient has exophthalmos and swelling in front of the neck, which moves upward
 on swallowing but doesn't move on protruding tongue.
 • The swelling in front of the neck on the patient's examination is most likely goiter.
 • In the given image, proptosis of the eye is shown.
 • The most common cause of unilateral proptosis in children is orbital cellulitis and in adults is thyroid
 ophthalmopathy.
 • The most common cause of bilateral proptosis in children is neuroblastoma and leukemia (chloroma)
 and in adults is thyroid ophthalmopathy.
 Option A: Orbital cellulitis
 • Orbital cellulitis is the infection of the orbital cavity.
 • It can be caused by bacteria, viruses or fungi.
 • It usually infects the soft tissues present in the orbital cavity.
 • This is not a case of orbital cellulitis.
 Option B: Preseptal cellulitis
 • The preseptal cellulitis is also known as periorbital cellulitis.
 • It is the infection of the frontal portion of the eyelid.
 • It doesn't involve the orbit.
 • This is not a case of preseptal cellulitis.
 Option D: Capillary hemangioma
 • Capillary hemangioma is the benign tumour of the orbit.
 • It usually affects infants.
 • It is caused due to incomplete blood vessel formation.
 • This is not a case of capillary hemangioma

Subject: Ophthalmology
QuestionText: A 5-year-old was brought with the complaints of protrusion of the right eye with no h/o fever. CT scan showed a well-defined mass in the orbit with an irregular border and adjacent bony destruction. Biopsy showed small, round cells which were positive for Desmin in immunohistochemistry. What is the probable diagnosis?
Explanation:
• "Rhabdomyosarcoma" is the probable diagnosis based on the given information.
 • Rhabdomyosarcoma is a malignant tumor that arises from embryonal cells of skeletal muscle origin. It
 is the most common soft tissue sarcoma in children and can occur in various locations, including the
 orbit. The clinical presentation of an orbital rhabdomyosarcoma often includes proptosis (protrusion of
 the eye), without a history of fever.
 • The CT scan findings of a well-defined mass in the orbit with an irregular border and adjacent bony
 destruction are consistent with the characteristics of an aggressive tumor. Rhabdomyosarcoma can
 invade and destroy surrounding tissues, including adjacent bone.
 • The biopsy findings of small, round cells that are positive for Desmin in immunohistochemistry support
 the diagnosis of rhabdomyosarcoma. Desmin is a marker commonly used to identify cells of skeletal
 muscle origin, and its positive staining suggests a muscular differentiation of the tumor cells.

Subject: Orthopedics
Explanation:
Brodie's abscess is a subacute or chronic bone abscess typically caused by Staphylococcus aureus inf
 ection. It often presents with localized pain and swelling. Radiographic findings may reveal a
 well-defined lytic lesion with a sclerotic border.
 • In children Brodie's abscess is most common in the metaphyseal area. Occurs before epiphyseal
 closure in a child.
 • Occurs before epiphyseal closure in a child.
 • In adults - more common in epiphysio-metaphyseal junction Occurs after epiphyseal closure in adults.
 • Occurs after epiphyseal closure in adults.
 • Most common sites - upper tibia, lower femur.
 • Most common organism - Staphylococcus aureus.
 • Most common age - 2nd decade.
 • Pathophysiology - Low virulence of the organism and good host resistance leads to confirming the
 infection. Cavity is formed. On aspiration sterile pus is seen - due to good host resistance.
 • Cavity is formed.
 • On aspiration sterile pus is seen - due to good host resistance.
 • Occurs before epiphyseal closure in a child.
 • Occurs after epiphyseal closure in adults.
 • Cavity is formed.
 • On aspiration sterile pus is seen - due to good host resistance.

Subject: Orthopedics
Explanation:
• Enchondromas are benign hyaline cartilage tumors of unknown etiology occurring in bones of
 endochondral origin.
 • Although they can develop in other bones as well, these tumors are most frequently found in the tiny
 bones of the hands and feet.
 • When multiple enchondromas manifest in a unilateral pattern, the ailment is known as Ollier's disease.
 • In the given X-ray growth of some benign tumors made of cartilage tissue near the tiny bones of the
 hand is observed

Subject: Orthopedics
Explanation:
• Based on clinical presentation and the X-ray film provided, the most likely diagnosis is osteoclastoma
 or giant cell tumor. 
• Osteoclastoma is a benign bone tumor that typically presents as a painful, gradually enlarging mass,
 most commonly located in the epiphyseal region of long bones.

The X-ray film shows characteristic features of osteoclastoma, including eccentric lytic lesions with well-defined borders and thinning of the cortex. These findings are typical of osteoclastoma and help differ
 entiate it from other bone tumors. Additionally, the presence of a
 soap bubble appearance, caused by the multiple small cystic spaces within the tumor, is a
 characteristic feature of osteoclastoma.
 • Osteoclastoma, also known as giant cell tumor, is a locally aggressive benign bone tumor that
 primarily affects the epiphyses of long bones.
 • Patients often present with a gradually progressive swelling around the affected joint, commonly seen
 in the distal femur, proximal tibia, and distal radius. Pain and restricted joint movement may also be
 present.
 • Pain and restricted joint movement may also be present.
 • Imaging Findings: X-ray imaging typically shows eccentric lytic lesions with well-defined borders and
 thinning of the cortex. The presence of a soap bubble appearance, caused by multiple small cystic
 spaces within the tumor, is a characteristic feature. Magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) can help assess
 the extent of the tumor and its proximity to critical structures.
 • The presence of a soap bubble appearance, caused by multiple small cystic spaces within the tumor,
 is a characteristic feature.
 • Magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) can help assess the extent of the tumor and its proximity to critical
 structures.
 • Histological Features: Osteoclastoma consist of a mixture of giant cells and mononuclear stromal
 cells. The giant cells are multinucleated and have abundant eosinophilic cytoplasm, while the stromal
 cells are mononuclear and spindle-shaped. The tumor cells are positive for CD68 and negative for
 CD34.
 • The giant cells are multinucleated and have abundant eosinophilic cytoplasm, while the stromal cells
 are mononuclear and spindle-shaped.
 • The tumor cells are positive for CD68 and negative for CD34.
 • Treatment: The mainstay of treatment is surgical excision. Curettage, with or without bone grafting, is
 commonly performed. In some cases, adjuvant therapies such as cryotherapy or phenol application
 may be used to reduce the risk of recurrence. In advanced cases or when surgery is not feasible,
 options like denosumab, a RANK ligand inhibitor, can be considered.

 is rare but can occur in a small percentage of cases.

Subject: Orthopedics
Explanation:
• Given the X-ray image showing a soap bubble appearance indicative of a giant cell tumor (GCT).
 Extended curettage involves the thorough removal of the tumor along with the surrounding affected
 tissue, followed by filling the resultant cavity with a bone graft. In the case of giant cell tumors (GCTs),
 extended curettage is the preferred treatment approach to achieve local control and decrease the risk
 of recurrence.
 • Treatment Options:
 • Extended curettage and bone grafting: This is the primary treatment for most GCTs.
 • Denosumab: Denosumab, a monoclonal antibody, is used as a treatment option for unresectable or
 recurrent GCTs. It helps reduce tumor size and control symptoms.
 • Radiation therapy: Radiation therapy may be used as an adjuvant treatment after surgery or as a
 palliative option for unresectable or recurrent cases.
 • Surgical stabilization: In cases of significant structural compromise or fractures, surgical stabilization
 techniques like internal fixation with plates and screws or intramedullary nailing may be employed.
 Page 25
 88
• Salvage procedures: Extensive or recurrent GCTs may require salvage procedures such as en bloc
 resection (removal of the tumor and surrounding tissues in one piece) or joint replacement.
 • Adjuvant chemotherapy: Adjuvant chemotherapy is rarely used for GCTs, typically in cases of
 metastatic disease or extensive unresectable tumors.

Subject: Orthopedics
Explanation:
• Adamantinoma is a rare primary bone tumor that typically affects the tibia (shinbone). It arises from
 the epithelial tissue within the bone. Adamantinoma is most commonly seen in the diaphysis (shaft) of
 the tibia, but it can also occur in other long bones.
 • Clinical Presentation:
 • Adamantinoma most commonly presents in young to middle-aged adults, typically between the ages
 of 20 and 40.
 • The most common site of involvement is the tibia, particularly the diaphysis (shaft) region.
 • Patients often present with localized pain, swelling, and a palpable mass in the affected bone.
 • Rarely, pathological fractures can occur.
 • Histopathology:
 • Adamantinoma is characterized by the presence of biphasic cellular components: epithelial-like cells
 and fibrous or osteofibrous stroma.
 • The epithelial-like cells form glandular or tubular structures resembling the enamel organ of
 developing teeth.
 • The stroma is composed of fibrous tissue with varying degrees of osteoid or bone formation.
 • Microscopic examination and immunohistochemistry are used to confirm the diagnosis.
 • Radiological Findings:
 • On X-ray, Adamantinoma typically presents as a lytic lesion within the affected bone, often with
 associated cortical thinning or expansion.
 • The lesion may exhibit a "soap-bubble" appearance due to areas of bone destruction and reactive
 bone formation.
 • Magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) can provide detailed information about the extent of the tumor and
 its relationship to surrounding structures.
 • Treatment:Surgical resection is the mainstay of treatment for Adamantinoma.

 x-ray shows a locally aggressive lesion arising from the interphalangeal joint. There is extensive
 calcification (mottled appearance). These features are suggestive of chondrosarcoma.
 • Chondrosarcoma is a malignant bone tumor arising from the cartilage cells.
 • It may arise in normal bone (primary chondrosarcoma), or a pre-existing cartilaginous tumor such as
 enchondroma (secondary chondrosarcoma). It may arise in any bone but is common in flat bones such
 as the scapula, pelvis, and ribs.
 • Diagnosis: It occurs commonly in adults between 30-60 years of age, and is rare in children. The
 tumor has a wide spectrum of aggressiveness; from low-grade malignant to highly malignant.
 • Metastasis occurs through the blood vessels, commonly in the lungs. Presenting symptoms are pain
 and swelling, often of long duration. X-ray shows erosion of the cortex and bone destruction. The tumor
 matrix may have mottled calcification, typical of cartilaginous tumors.
 • Diagnosis is confirmed by a biopsy. Treatment depends upon the behavior of the tumor. Amputation is
 necessary for most tumors. In some low-grade tumors, after proper assessment, wide resection of the
 tumor is done (limb-saving surgery). The role of chemotherapy and radiotherapy is doubtful

 Trichuris trichiura infection:
 • Transmission: Trichuris trichiura is primarily transmitted through ingestion of food or water

 • Clinical features: Infection with Trichuris trichiura can cause a condition called trichuriasis. Symptoms
 can vary from mild to severe and may include abdominal pain, diarrhea, bloody stools, anemia, weight

 provided image.

 presence of Trichuris trichiura eggs. The eggs can be visualized under a microscope and their
 characteristic shape and features can aid in the diagnosis of the infection.

 lamblia infection.
 • Giardia lamblia, also known as Giardia intestinalis, is a flagellated protozoan parasite that commonly

 • The life cycle of Giardia lamblia involves two main stages: The trophozoite The cyst. The trophozoites
 are the motile, active form of the parasite, while the cysts are the infective form that can survive outside

 typically attach to the intestinal epithelium using a sucking disk mechanism This attachment disrupts

 and watery diarrhea. The trophozoites can be seen as pear-shaped organisms with characteristic

 • Giardiasis, the infection caused by Giardia lamblia, is characterized by chronic or intermittent

 • Treatment for giardiasis usually involves antimicrobial therapy, such as metronidazole or tinidazole, to
 eliminate the parasite and alleviate symptoms.
 • Good hygiene practices, including clean water sources and proper sanitation, are essential in
 preventing the transmission of Giardia lamblia and reducing the risk of infection.

Subject: Microbiology
QuestionText: Please identify the organism depicted in the provided image's life cycle.
Explanation:
• Toxoplasma gondii has a complex life cycle involving definitive hosts (felids) and intermediate hosts,
 including humans.
 • In the definitive host, such as domestic cats, the cycle begins when they ingest tissue cysts from
 intermediate hosts or oocysts shed in the feces of infected cats.
 • Once inside the cat's small intestine, the parasite undergoes asexual replication, forming rapidly
 dividing tachyzoites.
 • Sexual replication then occurs within the intestinal cells, leading to the formation of male and female
 gametes.
 • Fertilization of these gametes results in the production of zygotes, which develop into oocysts. These
 oocysts are shed in the cat's feces, contaminating the environment.
 • Transmission of Toxoplasma gondii can occur if an intermediate host, such as a rodent or bird, is
 consumed by a felid. In this case, the tissue cysts containing bradyzoites are ingested by the cat,
 allowing the parasite to complete its sexual replication within the cat's intestine and restart the cycle.

QuestionText: What is the medication used to treat the infection caused by the cestode egg depicted in the image?

Subject: Microbiology
QuestionText: What organism is responsible for causing the condition depicted in the image?
Explanation:
The causative organism for the condition shown in the image of a hydatid cyst in the liver is option a.
 Echinococcus granulosus.

 echinococcosis. It primarily affects the liver, but can also involve other organs such as the lungs and

QuestionText: What is the most likely diagnosis for a 54-year-old farmer from Ohio who visits a physician due to a persistent cough? A chest x-ray reveals the presence of a mass lesion accompanied by hilar lymphadenopathy. Upon conducting a biopsy of the mass and staining it with lactophenol cotton blue, multiple yeast forms are observed within macrophages.
Explanation:
• Based on the presentation and the staining findings, the most likely diagnosis for the 54-year-old
 farmer from Ohio with a chronic cough and chest x-ray showing a mass lesion with hilar
 lymphadenopathy is Histoplasmosis. This fungal infection is commonly associated with farmers and
 individuals who are exposed to bird or bat droppings, as the fungus Histoplasma capsulatum is found in
 soil enriched with such organic matter. The presentation of chronic cough, lung mass, and hilar
 lymphadenopathy aligns with pulmonary histoplasmosis

Subject: Microbiology
QuestionText: In September, a patient from Uttarakhand presented with fever and muscle pain. The patient was initially treated with IV ceftriaxone, but it proved ineffective. Subsequently, during examination, the patient observed skin lesions (specifically, black eschar) as shown in the accompanying image. What is your diagnosis for this case?
Explanation:
The correct diagnosis for the patient presenting with fever, myalgia, and cutaneous lesions (black
 eschar) in Uttarakhand during September is Scrub typhus (Option A).
 • Scrub typhus is a bacterial infection caused by the Orientia tsutsugamushi bacterium. It is transmitted
 to humans through the bite of infected chiggers (mites) found in areas with dense vegetation. Scrub
 typhus is commonly seen in rural areas, particularly in regions with heavy vegetation, such as forests or
 grasslands.
 • The clinical presentation of scrub typhus includes fever, headache, myalgia (muscle pain), and a
 characteristic eschar at the site of the chigger bite. The eschar is a black, necrotic lesion surrounded by
 an area of inflammation. It typically appears within a week of the chigger bite and is an important
 diagnostic clue for scrub typhus

QuestionText: Which congenital heart disease exhibits equivalent saturation levels across all heart chambers among the options listed below?
Explanation: Correct Choice: B → Total anomalous pulmonary venous return is cyanotic heart disease where the pulmonary vein fails to enter into the left atrium and drains abnormally into the systemic venous system resulting in mixed blood into the systemic circulation. → This results in failure of closure of foramen result into blood flow from right atrium to left follow by blood flow to left ventricle than to systemic circulation. → This result is equal blood saturation in all four chambers of heart and systemic circulation. Incorrect Choices: Option A. Tetralogy of fallot: In Tetralogy of fallot, there is ventricular septal defect, overriding aorta, pul monary stenosis and right ventricular hypertrophy. → Thus, the failure in closure of foramen ovale, right to left resulting in mixed blood in left heart and unoxygenated blood in right heart. → Hence all four chamber don't have same Oxygen concentration. Option C: Tricuspid atresia: In Tricuspid atresia, there is decreased blood flow from right atrium to ventr icule causing right to left shunt and pulmonary oligemia. Option D: Transposition of great arteries. → In transposition of great arteries, right ventricle ends at right aorta which carries deoxygenated blood from right ventricle to the tissue while left ventricle drains into pulmonary artery. → Hence two separate system are created. Deoxygenated blood is circulated in systemic circulation and oxygenated in pulmonary circulation. → D-looped transposition of the great arteries (TGA). A, Diagram of d-TGA, with the main pulmonary artery (MPA) arising from the left ventricle (LV) and the aorta (Ao) arising from the right ventricle (RV).

Subject: Cardiology
QuestionText: A 24-year-old female comes in for a prenatal examination and is presently taking lithium for her bipolar disorder. It is necessary to discontinue this medication to avoid the potential occurrence of which of the subsequent fetal conditions?
Explanation: Correct choice: A Explanation: → Ebstein anomaly of the tricuspid valve is a congenital defect characterized by downward displacement of the insertion of septal and posterior leaflets. Such displacement leads to tricuspid regurgitation secondary to lack of coaptation of the leaflets. It is the improper development of the tricuspid valve in the first eight weeks of fetal growth. In this anomaly the tricuspid valve and the right ventricle are involved. The cardinal symptoms in Ebstein's anomaly are cyanosis, right-sided heart failure, arrhythmias, and sudden cardiac death. → The risks of having a baby while taking lithium while pregnant or breastfeeding will cause heart defects. Taking lithium in early pregnancy can increase the risk that the baby's heart might not develop properly. Through ultrasound imaging during pregnancy, will be able to diagnose Ebstein's anomaly before the baby is born. → There has been concern that exposure to lithium early in pregnancy may be associated with a marked increase in the risk of Ebstein's anomaly (a right ventricular outflow tract obstruction defect)in infants and overall congenital cardiac defects. Lithium is an effective treatment in pregnancy and postpartum for the prevention of relapse in bipolar disorder. Hemodynamics → The tricuspid valve anomaly results in obstruction to forward flow of blood as well as regurgitation of blood from the right ventricle into the right atrium. In addition, there is a large part of the right ventricle that is atrialized as a result of downward displacement of the tricuspid → valve attachment. This atrialized right ventricle contracts with the rest of the ventricle and does not allow effective forward flow into the pulmonary circulation. The right atrium progressively dilates, to accommodate the extra volume. The foramen ovale may be patent or there is an atrial septal defect allowing a right to left shunt to occur. This results in cyanosis. The greater the tricuspid valve displacement, the more the cyanosis. Diagnosis and Treatment → The diagnosis can be easily confirmed by echocardiography, which can not only identify the anomaly but also indicate the severity. Surgical treatment consists in obliterating the atrialized portion of the right ventricle and repairing the tricuspid valve. Incorrect choices Opion BVACTERLl defects or VACTERL association is a disorder that affects many body systems. It st ands for vertebral defects, anal atresia, cardiac defects, tracheo-esophageal fistula, renal anomalies, a nd limb abnormalities. Tracheoesophageal fistula is the most common birth defect in the VACTERL association. Option C: Coarctation of the aorta is a birth defect(congenital) in which a part of the aorta is narrower t han usual. It is most commonly occurring just beyond the left subclavian artery. It results from abnorma lities in development of the aorta prior to birth. Many people have no symptoms and the condition often isn't detected until adulthood. The most common symptom in adults is high blood pressure. Option D: Supravalvular aortic stenosis (SVAS) is a rare congenital heart defect in which the left ventric le is narrowed below the level of the aortic valve resulting in obstruction to blood flow out of the heart. T he condition is also known as subaortic stenosis. Surgery is the primary treatment for supravalvar aorti c stenosis.

Subject: Neurology
QuestionText: What is the most common cause of seizures in a 2-year-old child?
Explanation: Answer Option C. Febrile seizures are the most common type of seizures in children aged 6 months to 5 years. They are convulsions that occur in response to a fever, usually above 38°C (100.4° F). Febrile seizures are more common in boys than girls and tend to occur in children with a family history of febrile seizures. Most febrile seizures are harmless and do not cause any long-term damage. They usually last less tha n 5 minutes and do not recur within 24 hours. However, in some cases, febrile seizures may be a sign of a serious underlying condition, such as meningitis or encephalitis. Therefore, it is important to s eek medical attention if a child has a febrile seizure. Trauma to the head, malnutrition, and neurological deficit can also cause seizures in children, but they are fewer common causes than febrile seizures. Trauma to the head may lead to post-traumatic seizures, malnutrition may cause metabolic abnormaliti es that can lead to seizures, and neurological deficits may result from various conditions, such as cere bral palsy or brain malformations. Incorrect Choices: a. Trauma to the head: While trauma to the head can cause seizures in children, it is less common tha n febrile seizures. b. Malnutrition: Malnutrition can cause metabolic abnormalities that may lead to seizures, but it is less c ommon than febrile seizures. d. Neurological deficit: Neurological deficit may cause seizures but is less common than febrile seizure s. QuestionText: Which of the following are the causes of neonatal seizures? 1. Hypocalcemia 2. Hyponatremia 3. Hypomagnesemia 4. Hypoglycemia (or) What are the factors that can lead to seizures in newborns? 1. Hypocalcemia 2. Hyponatremia 3. Hypomagnesemia 4. Hypoglycemia
Explanation: Correct Choice: B Some of the electrolyte abnormalities that can be responsible for neonatal seizures include: Hypocalcemia: Low levels of calcium in the blood can cause neonatal seizures. Calcium is essential for normal nerve function, and when its levels are decreased, the excitability of neurons may increase, lea ding to seizure activity. Hyponatremia: Low levels of sodium in the blood can also be associated with neonatal seizures. Sodiu m is vital for maintaining proper nerve function and the balance of fluids in and around cells. A deficiency in sodium can lead to neuronal hyperexcitability and seizures. Hypomagnesemia: Magnesium is another important electrolyte that affects nerve function. Low levels o f magnesium can lead to increased neuronal excitability and, in some cases, seizures. Hypoglycemia: Although glucose is not an electrolyte, it is an essential energy source for the brain. Ne onates with low blood sugar levels (hypoglycemia) may exhibit seizure activity due to the brain's inadeq uate energy supply. Other Causes Hypokalemia: Low levels of potassium in the blood can contribute to neonatal seizures. Potassium is cr ucial for maintaining the resting membrane potential of neurons, and its deficiency can lead to increase d neuronal excitability. Hypernatremia: Extremely high levels of sodium in the blood may also be associated with neonatal seiz ures. Such electrolyte imbalances can disrupt normal cellular function and lead to seizures. It is a Gram-positive bacterium that can be transmitted from mother to newborn during childbirth. Neonates are particularly susceptible to GBS infection because their immune systems are not fully developed, and their blood-brain barrier is not as effective at preventing bacterial invasion of the central nervous system. Explanation for the options: Option A: Diazepam: Diazepam is a benzodiazepine commonly used as an initial treatment for acute s eizures and status epilepticus. It has a rapid onset of action and can be administered intravenously, intr amuscularly, or rectally. It works by enhancing the inhibitory effects of gamma-aminobutyric acid (GAB A) in the brain, leading to a reduction in seizure activity. Option B: Phenytoin: Phenytoin is an anticonvulsant medication used to control seizures. However, it is not typically the first-line choice for the treatment of acute seizures or status epilepticus due to its slow onset of action. Phenytoin requires slow intravenous administration to avoid adverse effects and is mor e commonly used for long-term seizure control. Option C: Phenobarbitone: Phenobarbitone is a barbiturate medication used for the treatment of seizur es. While it is effective in controlling seizures, it is not typically the first choice for the initial managemen t of acute seizures or status epilepticus in children. It has a slower onset of action compared to benzodi azepines and may cause sedation and respiratory depression. Option D: Thiopentone sodium: Thiopentone sodium, also known as thiopental, is an intravenous anest hetic agent. It is not commonly used as the first-line treatment for acute seizures or status epilepticus. I ts main use is in anesthesia induction and maintenance. In the given scenario, with the child presenting with continuing seizures for the past 30 minutes, the im mediate goal is to stop the ongoing seizure activity to prevent further complications. Diazepam, a benz odiazepine, is the preferred initial treatment in such situations due to its rapid onset of action and effect iveness in terminating seizures. It can be administered intravenously or rectally. Diazepam acts as a po tent sedative and anticonvulsant by enhancing the effects of GABA, thereby suppressing excessive ne uronal activity and terminating seizures. Once the seizures are controlled, further evaluation and mana gement can be planned to address the underlying cause and prevent recurrence. Solution for Question 14: Correct Option C: It is associated with facial angiofibroma Page 15 1229 (cid:127) The given image shows discrete nodules over the iris which are Lisch nodules seen in neurofibromatosis 1. (cid:127) It is associated with facial angiofibroma (False) (cid:127) Facial angiofibromas, also known as adenoma sebaceum, are skin lesions characterized by red papules or nodules that commonly occur in the central face, particularly the cheeks and nose. They are associated with tuberous sclerosis, not with the given image of discrete nodules on the iris.

Subject: Neurology
QuestionText: What is the likely diagnosis for adolescent girls who experience instances of dropping objects from their hands, particularly in the morning and during exams, without any history of loss of consciousness, and with a cousin diagnosed with epilepsy, when an EEG shows signs of epileptic spikes?
Explanation: Correct Option A. (cid:127) JME is a common form of epilepsy that typically begins in adolescence. It is characterized by brief, involuntary muscle jerks or myoclonic seizures, which can result in the dropping of objects. These seizures often occur in the morning upon waking and can be triggered by fatigue, stress, or sleep deprivation. The presence of epileptic spikes on the EEG further supports the diagnosis of JME. Incorrect Option Option B. Atypical absence: (cid:127) Atypical absence seizures typically present with a brief loss of awareness, accompanied by subtle motor manifestations such as eye blinking or slight movements. They are usually not associated with dropping objects as described in the case. Option C. Choreo-athetoid epilepsy: (cid:127) Choreo-athetoid movements refer to involuntary, irregular, and writhing movements. However, chores-athetoid epilepsy is a rare form of epilepsy and typically presents with a combination of seizures and abnormal movement patterns. It is not the most probable diagnosis based on the given information. Option D. Centrotemporal spikes: (cid:127) Centrotemporal spikes, also known as benign rolandic epilepsy, are characterized by seizures that primarily occur during sleep and involve the face, mouth, and throat muscles. Dropping objects is not a typical feature of this epilepsy syndrome Solution for Question 18: Correct Option D. (cid:127) Decorticate and decerebrate posturing are abnormal motor responses observed in patients with severe brain injury, but they are not typically seen in brain death. In brain death, there is a complete loss of cerebral and brainstem function, including motor responses. Therefore, the absence of decorticate and decerebrate posturing is consistent with brain death. Incorrect Option Option A. Normal blood pressure without pharmacological support: (cid:127) In brain death, the autonomic centers in the brain that regulate blood pressure and heart rate are typically affected. As a result, maintaining normal blood pressure without the need for pharmacological Page 17 1231 support is not observed in brain-dead patients. Hypotension or instability of blood pressure requiring vasopressor support is more commonly seen. Option B. Positive spinal reflexes on stimulation: (cid:127) Brain death is characterized by the irreversible loss of all cerebral and brainstem functions. While the brainstem reflexes may still be present and may include spinal reflexes, they do not override the absence of higher brain functions. Therefore, positive spinal reflexes on stimulation are observed in brain death and are not a differentiating feature. Option C. Sweating and tachycardia: (cid:127) Sweating and tachycardia are autonomic responses regulated by the brainstem. In brain death, the disruption of the autonomic centers in the brainstem leads to the loss of these responses. Therefore, sweating and tachycardia are not seen in brain-dead patients and can help support the diagnosis. Solution for Question 19: Correct Option B. (cid:127) Focal clonic seizures are a type of seizure characterized by rhythmic jerking movements in a specific area of the body. These seizures typically involve one part of the body, such as an arm, leg, or face, and can be repetitive and rhythmic in nature. The movements are usually brief and may occur in clusters. Incorrect Option Option A. Focal tonic seizure: (cid:127) Focal tonic seizures are characterized by sustained muscle contractions or stiffening of a specific body part. This does not match the observed movements in the neonate. Option C. Spasms: (cid:127) Spasms, also known as infantile spasms or West syndrome, are a specific type of seizure characterized by sudden, brief, and symmetric muscle contractions. These contractions typically involve the trunk and limbs and may cause a characteristic head drop or bending forward. The observed movements in the neonate do not resemble spasms. Option D. Subtle seizure: (cid:127) Subtle seizures, also known as non-convulsive seizures or electroclinical seizures, are seizures that do not have obvious clinical manifestations. They may manifest as subtle changes in behavior, eye movements, or body tone. However, the observed movements in the neonate are more consistent with focal clonic seizures than subtle seizures. Solution for Question 20: Correct Option A: Page 18 1232 (cid:127) This option is the correct answer. After starting antibiotics for bacterial meningitis, the child should be isolated for at least 24 hours. This allows sufficient time for the antibiotics to take effect, reducing the bacterial load and decreasing the risk of transmission to others. After this period, the child is generally considered to be less contagious.

Subject: Orthopedics
QuestionText: Which of the given structures below is associated with the highlighted structure (shown in red) in the image?

Subject: Orthopedics
QuestionText: For which type of nerve injuries is the instrument depicted below employed?
Explanation:
A radial nerve palsy splint(as shown in the image) is an orthotic device used to support and
 immobilize the hand and wrist in cases of radial nerve palsy.
 • Radial nerve palsy refers to damage or dysfunction of the radial nerve, which can result in weakness
 or paralysis of the muscles controlled by the nerve.
 • The radial nerve innervates muscles responsible for wrist extension, thumb extension, and finger
 extension.
 • The purpose of a radial nerve palsy splint is to provide support and prevent further damage to the
 affected area while allowing for healing and rehabilitation. The splint typically extends from the forearm
 to the hand, with a rigid or semi-rigid component that keeps the wrist and fingers functional.

Subject: Orthopedics
QuestionText: What condition is caused by nerve injury?
Explanation:
The following image shows an ulnar claw due to Ulnar nerve injury or compression.
 • There is hyperextension at the metacarpophalangeal joints and flexion at the proximal and distal
 interphalangeal joints of the 4th and 5th digits due to the paralysis of the 3rd and 4th lubricants, which
 are supplied by the ulnar nerve.
 There are various methods for testing ulnar nerve injuries:
 • Card test
 • Igawa test
• Book test
 • Wartenberg test
 The Ulnar paradox explains that the amount of clawing depends on the site of the ulnar nerve injury
 • Injury around elbow: High ulnar nerve palsy, less clawing
 • Injury at wrist: Low ulnar nerve palsy, more clawing.

Subject: Orthopedics
QuestionText: A 30-year-old male with extreme pain in his hand was brought to the emergency department after a road traffic accident. The X-ray shows Holstein-Lewis fracture. What results from the nerve injury?
Explanation:
The X-ray displays Holstein-Lewis fracture which leads to higher radial nerve palsy resulting in wrist
 drop
 • Holstein-Lewis fracture (most common) Occurs in the lower 3rd of the humerus. Oblique fracture with
 entrapped radial nerve. Axonotmesis or Neurotmesis (as this is a trauma)
 • Occurs in the lower 3rd of the humerus.
 • Oblique fracture with entrapped radial nerve.
 • Axonotmesis or Neurotmesis (as this is a trauma)
 • Occurs in the lower 3rd of the humerus.
 • Oblique fracture with entrapped radial nerve.
 • Axonotmesis or Neurotmesis (as this is a trauma)
 This condition involves:
 • Loss of elbow extension
 • Loss of wrist extension
 • Loss of finger extension
 • Loss of sensations over the posterior part of the forearm and the dorsum of the hand
OptionD: Sensations over the posterior part of the forearm are preserved

Subject: ENT
QuestionText: What potential diagnosis can be made for a patient who has a firm, tender, gradually enlarging mass located below the ear, as depicted in the image provided?
Explanation:
• A Patient presented with, firm, tender, slow-growing mass underneath the ear, suggests a parotid
 abscess. 
• The parotid gland is a salivary organ found in front of the ear and when it becomes infected, it can
 result in the arrangement of an abscess. 
• The diagnosis of a parotid abscess can be affirmed by clinical examination, and imaging ponders may
 be utilized to evaluate the degree of the abscess and direct treatment. Treatment typically includes
 antibiotics, incision and drainage, and pain management.

Subject: ENT
QuestionText: Please identify the structure shown in this endoscopic image of the nasopharynx.
Explanation:
Correct Option: C Option C: Fossa of Rosenmuller: The Fossa of Rosenmuller, also known as the Ros
 enmuller's fossa, is a depression or recess located behind the torus tubarius (the prominence of tissue 
in the lateral wall of the nasopharynx). It is a normal anatomical feature.
 Other options
 Option A: Sinus of Morgagni: The Sinus of Morgagni refers to small recesses or pouches located at the
 base of the epiglottis in the larynx. These are considered normal anatomical structures.
 Option B: Passavant's Ridge: Passavant's ridge is a
 mucosal fold that forms during swallowing in the nasopharynx. It serves as a
protective mechanism to prevent food or fluids from entering the nasopharynx during swallowing.
 Option D: Rathke's Pouch: Rathke's pouch is a structure during embryonic development that gives rise 
to the anterior pituitary gland. It is not typically visible in the nasopharynx.

Subject: ENT
QuestionText: The image given below is from a chronic smoker’s oral examination. Identify the lesion given.
Explanation:
• Leukoplakia presents as white patch in the oral cavity mucosa. Sites involved: Buccal mucosa and
 oral commissures are the most common sites. It may however involve the floor of the mouth,
 gingivobuccal sulcus and mucosal surface of the lip. Buccal mucosa is the most common site in India.
 • WHO defined leukoplakia as a clinical white patch that cannot be characterized clinically or
 pathologically as any other disease. It is a clinical definition and does not take pathology into
 consideration. Other white lesions of the oral mucosa, i.e. lichen planus, discoid lupus erythematosus,
 white spongy nevus and candidiasis excluded.

Subject: ENT
QuestionText: The image below depicts oral cavity findings in a patient who is a smoker. What would be your preferred course of action for managing this patient?
Explanation:
• In the given scenario, the patient presents with a wrinkled white patch on the ventral surface of the
 tongue, which is consistent with leukoplakia. Leukoplakia is a potentially premalignant condition
 characterized by persistent white patches or plaques that cannot be wiped away and cannot be
 attributed to any other known cause.
 • Since the patient is a smoker, it increases the suspicion of the lesion being potentially malignant.
 Therefore, the next step in the management of this patient would be to immediately address the most
 significant risk factor, which is smoking. The patient should be advised to cease smoking as it plays a
 crucial role in the development and progression of oral cavity lesions.
OptionB: Cessation of smoking and do biopsy

Subject: ENT
QuestionText: Which of the following structures will the senior resident in the hospital not be able to see while performing the clinical examination depicted in the image below?
Explanation:
• Arytenoids: The arytenoids are not typically visible during posterior rhinoscopy. The examination
 primarily focuses on the posterior part of the nasal cavity, nasopharynx, and structures such as the
 adenoids and the torus tubarius. The arytenoids, on the other hand, are located in the larynx, which is
 not directly visualized during posterior rhinoscopy.

QuestionText: A child presents to you with the chief complaint of loud breathing. Upon examination, you observe the child experiencing inspiratory stridor, and the laryngeal examination reveals the following findings. Based on the information provided, what is the most probable diagnosis?

Subject: Dermatology
QuestionText: What condition is indicated by the skin appearance?
Explanation:
The injury appeared within the picture appears to be an acanthosis nigricans which is a common
 skin growth in people who are overweight or obese. 
• Whereas skin tags are not particular to any specific condition, they are more commonly seen in
 people with metabolic disorders, a cluster of conditions that incorporates weight, high blood weight,
 high blood sugar, and abnormal cholesterol levels. Subsequently, the patient is most likely enduring
 metabolic syndrome.

Subject: Dermatology
QuestionText: What type of skin cancer is indicated by the lesion?
Explanation:
Malignant melanoma is a type of skin cancer that develops from melanocytes, the cells responsible
 for producing the pigment melanin. It often appears as an asymmetrical, irregularly bordered, and 
multi-colored lesion on the skin. The image given is likely to be malignant melanoma as it is consist
 ent with the ABCDE rule that is:
 • A - Asymmetry: Look for any irregularity in shape. Melanomas often have an asymmetrical shape,
 meaning one half doesn't match the other half.
 • B - Border: Check the edges of the lesion. Melanomas tend to have uneven, ragged, or blurred
 borders, unlike benign moles that typically have smooth, well-defined borders.
 • C - Color: Examine the color of the lesion. Melanomas may display various shades of brown,
 black, blue, red, or white within the same lesion. Uneven distribution or changes in color should
 raise concerns.
 • D - Diameter: Consider the size of the lesion. While melanomas can be small, they are typically
 larger than benign moles. Any mole or lesion larger than 6 millimeters (about the size of a pencil
 eraser) should be examined by a healthcare professional.
 • E - Evolution: Monitor the evolution or changes in the lesion over time. Melanomas often undergo
 changes in size, shape, color, or elevation. Rapid or significant changes should be evaluated
 promptly

Subject: Dermatology
QuestionText: What is the diagnosis based on the skin lesion?
Explanation:
The diagnosis based on the given image is Becker's naevus. Becker's naevus is a relatively
 common pigmented skin lesion that typically appears in adolescence or early adulthood.
 • It is characterized by a large, irregularly shaped patch or plaque with increased pigmentation.
 • Becker's naevus often occurs on the shoulder, upper arm, or trunk and tends to be more common
 in males.
 • It can vary in size and may exhibit hair growth (hypertrichosis) within the lesion.

QuestionText: A patient presents to the clinic with the lesion given in the image. He had a traumatic injury to the chest one year ago and the scar has grown beyond the boundary of the initial injury. What is the most likely diagnosis?
Explanation: Correct Option C - Keloid: Based on the provided history of a traumatic injury to the chest a year ago and the appearance of the l esion that is growing beyond the boundary of the initial injury, the most likely diagnosis is a keloid. Keloids are benign overgrowths of scar tissue that often form at the site of skin injury. Incorrect Choices: Option A - Hemangiomas: They are benign tumors of blood vessels. They are typically present from bir th or appear in early childhood and do not form in response to trauma. Option B - Hypertrophic scar: It is like a keloid and is a result of an overactive healing process. Howeve r, unlike keloids, they do not extend beyond the original wound boundaries. Option D - Neurofibromas: They are benign nerve sheath tumors. They are associated with genetic con ditions such as neurofibromatosis rather than trauma.

Subject: Surgery
QuestionText: What is the probable diagnosis based on the provided image of the anus? It typically arises from the obstruction and infection of an anal gland, leading to the accumulation of pus and fluid, consequently causing a highly distressing abscess in the vicinity of the anus.
Explanation: Correct Option: C: Perianal abscess → A perianal abscess is an anorectal abscess that occurs only in the perianal area. Inflammatory bowel illnesses, such as Crohn's disease, as well as trauma or malignant origins, can also be caused. Patients who have recurring or complicated abscesses should be tested for Crohn's disease. → A perianal abscess is a pus-filled boil that develops around the anus, rectum, or perineal area (the space between your genitals and anus). It occurs when one of your anus glands becomes clogged and infected. Pus and fluid accumulate in the anal gland, resulting in an excruciatingly painful abscess around your anus (the hole in your buttocks).

Subject: Pre Anesthesic
QuestionText: Which investigation yields the most precise prognostic information for predicting the risk of perioperative cardiac complications among the options provided?
Explanation: Correct Option B: Dobutamine stress echocardiography provides valuable information about the heart's function and blood flow under stress, making it a more accurate predictor of perioperative cardiac complications than the other options listed.

Subject: Pre Anesthesic
QuestionText: When assessing a patient before anesthesia, the oral cavity should be examined. The image provided below displays the examined cavity. It illustrates:
Explanation: Correct Option C: Mallampati class III indicates that only the base of the uvula is visible during the oral examination. This classification helps assess the difficulty of intubation.

Subject: Pre Anesthesic
QuestionText: What is the most effective approach for verifying the accuracy of intubation during laryngoscopy?
Explanation: Correct Option A: Capnography is the most reliable method for confirming correct intubation. It measures CO2 levels in exhaled air, providing immediate confirmation of tube placement. Other methods, while useful, are less definitive.

Subject: Protein And Amino Acids
QuestionText: What is the most accurate method for measuring glycated hemoglobin (HbA1c)?
Explanation:
• Glycated hemoglobin (HbA1c) is measured by ion exchange chromatography. This method is based
 on differences in charge. A mixture of charged proteins is analyzed over carboxymethylcellulose paper
 which is negatively charged. A positively charged resin bed separates negatively charged HbA1c. It has
 the advantage of separating hemoglobin variants into peaks. Some hemoglobin variants may interfere
 with results.
